WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 10.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, SEPTEMBER 1, 1881.	NO. 7


TO MY FRIEND.

Into my bosom dropped a star,
From out the Heaven's vast firmament,
And there so pure and bright it lay,
I scarce believed to me 'twas sent.
I clasped it close with wild delight,
I bathed its crimson rays with dew,
I wondered much, for love was blind,
But now I know, dear friend, 'twas you.

Into my bosom breathed a song,
From spheres celestial, pure and clear,
Its harmonies my soul entranced,
And life stood still its strains to hear.
My saddened heart it filled with joy,
Breathed o'er my spirit strength anew,
I wondered much, for love was dumb,
But now I know, sweet friend, 'twas you.

Into my bosom crept a flower,
Entwined around my heart to kiss,
Its glorious beauty charmed my eyes,
Its fragrance steeped each sense in bliss.
Perfumes of Araby the blest,
Exhaled from out its petals blue,
I wondered much, till love confessed,
That star, and song, and flower were you.
	ANONYMOUS.
SALT LAKE CITY, February 10, 1881


[Column 1]

A FEW INCIDENTS.

	Desiring to inspire faith in the minds of our
young people, I have decided to publish some
items from manuscript which I have in my
possession, that I think will be interesting to
the readers of the EXPONENT. It will also
give to the young some idea what their mothers
and the veteran women of this Church have
endured for the principles of this faith, or for
the Gospel's sake. These items are from the
writings of a lady now deceased, Louisa B.
Pratt of Beaver.
	"I have had deep experience in this kingdom:
having spent over 40 years in active service. I
was raised under the influence of the Episcopal
Church; continued steadfast in that faith until
I heard the fulness of the Everlasting Gospel,
revealed through the Prophet Joseph Smith in
this our day. And verily it was by the power
and goodness of God that it was made known
to me, for I would not believe the testimony of
the Elder who taught me, although I knew him
to be a man of candor and truth. I had known
the Scriptures from my childhood, and I knew
his teachings were in strict accordance with the
apostolic doctrines, yet I would not acknow-
ledge it to him. At length I resolved to know
independent of any human testimony. So with
all the energy with which my soul was endowed,
I cried unto the Lord, and suddenly the light
burst upon my heart like the sun breaking forth
from behind a dark cloud when the sky is
clothed in blackness; the Bible was in my hand
and had it been printed in golden capitals and
been of doubtful meaning it would not have
looked half so beautiful; joy unutterable, per-
meated my whole existence. One week passed
away, I was conscious of realizing more true
joy in that short time than in all the years of
my life, which numbered 35.
	We were living on the banks of Lake Erie,
enjoying all the comforts and many of the lux-
uries of life. Jackson County, Missouri was
then the centre of attraction in my mind. My
husband was an unbeliever. He invited a Bap-
tist preacher to visit us, hoping by powerful
arguments he would convince me of my error,

[Column 2]

as he listened to our discussion he saw the fal-
lacy and lameness of his reasoning, and dis-
cerned that the Bible was a sealed book to the
noted preacher. He went to Kirtland on pur-
pose to learn what he could of the origin of the
work which was creating such excitement
through the country. While there became
convinced of its truth and was baptised. He
returned, and testified to his astonished breth-
ren of the Presbyterian order of the great
things he had seen and heard, but they would
not believe. In process of time we found our-
selves﻿ identified with the Saints in Nauvoo; en-
listed hearts and hands to build the Temple. In
1843 Mr. Pratt was called on a mission to the
Pacific Isles. Our property was invested in
land, in the State of Indiana; there was no
ready means to be left for my support, with
four young children, all daughters. I felt that
I must nerve my heart to contend "against the
world, the flesh and the devil." The Saints
were poor, I must "paddle my own canoe." I
formed my plans and commenced in earnest;
great was my trust in God. I had never
learned to distrust men who had the reputation
of being just; but I found that even such ones
sometimes failed, while others were better than
their promises.
	I adopted a rule which I have ever since
found to be invaluable. I determined that my
word should be my bond, and though I promised
to my loss, I made that promise good. This
more than all else of my experience in battling
the storms of life alone, I wish published, for
the encouragement of numbers of my sisters
who like me are left to manage their own busi-
ness and provide for their families. I had
acquired a tolerable education in the govern-
ment schools in Lower Canada; was capable of
teaaching all the common branches, besides I
was a good seamstress. I was left in a small
log house, I immediately set about building a
framed house, buying the lumber on credit.
By the time the building was completed I had
won the reputation of being a punctual business
woman. I was proud of my house, lived in it
three winters, had to make a sacrifice of it when
we left Nauvoo; never realising but $15,00 in
exchange for the house and two City Lots.
Soon after my companion left home, serious
accidents happened to my children, one was
nearly crushed to death by the fall of a seat
loaded with people. The young children who
were with her ran through the crowd asking for
Elders to come and administer to the poor girl:
they did so, and she was brought home to me as
from the dead. I never thought of a Doctor,
but there was earnest pleading with Him who
has power to heal; and before the dawn of the
coming morn she was cheerful and able to re-
late the whole affair; and soon she was wholly
restored, and my heart comforted. The next
that happened the small pox was brought to
the place by the return of my brother-in-law
from Mass. coming up the Miss., on a Steamer.
Hearing of his return, I hurried with my four
children to the house to see him. My two
eldest had been vaccinated the other two had
not. I myself had been, likewise my sister, 17
years before. I stood by his bedside, gazing
upon him with wonder; the children standing
by. He told me to be on my guard, he feared
what it might be; and I withdrew. The neigh-
bors were alarmed when they found I had
been exposed, they would not venture them-
selves into my house.
	The fears of my friends excited faith in me

[Column 3]

to resist the power of the destroyer. I reasoned
with them, that they should help me to have
faith in the power of God, that He would not
suffer such an affliction to come on me when
my husband had been called on a foreign mis-
sion to preach the Gospel of Christ, and left
me to bear the burdens of life alone. My faith
increased as I uttered the words, and I declared
in the name of the Lord that the small pox
should not come under my roof that I would
claim the right to resist it by the power of
God! one child I watched closely, praying
continually that I might not have to recall my
words. At the close of the 9th day I saw symp-
toms of fever, a tremor in the neck. I laid
the girl in the upper room, and struggled with
my determination. At length I discovered on
the knee and fingers of the child nine very
small pimples; still I did not waver or doubt.
Seeing a brother on the street whom I knew
would not be afraid, I took the child to him
to be examined, he having been acquainted
with the disease pronounced it an attack, in
mild form, that it would not enlarge, nor do
her harm, but would prevent in all future her
taking the disease. The same proved true to
the letter as she was afterwards exposed in Cali-
fornia when the tyrant was making fearful
ravages. Then did I render thanks that my
daughter was proof against the direst of all ail-
ments.


EXPRESSION OF THANKS.

	DEAR EDITOR: Every number I receive of
your valuable paper seems to me more and
more interesting and instructive. The noble
ladies who write for the paper I for one feel
to thank for their kindness in making it so
truly valuable in the cause of truth and right-
eousness, and I sincerely wish it was patroniz-
ed by all who love the truth. I also thank
that lady who gave us such a graphic descrip-
tion of what our little children are doing in
the Primary Association. It was very pleas-
ing to read it, I assure you. I also have wished
many times to thank, through the EXPONENT,
those noble ladies of the Woman's Suffrage Asso-
ciation for their good and generous feelings to-
ward the women of Utah. I know we all
really appreciate their acts of kindness towards
us, and I feel my sentiments are those of many
others of our people.
	I, too, often thought with Sister Helen Mar
Whitney, that if the Book of Mormon was a 
little larger in print many would read it who
do not to-day. This has been a source of re-
gret to myself many times as my sight is fail-
ing me, and I always delight to read that book,
and think it a great blessing to live in such an
age, when truth has sprung out of the ground,
and righteousness has looked down from hea-
ven, for the good of a fallen world. Then let
us as sister struggle on in the cause of truth,
and never become weary in well doing, for in
due time we shall reap, if we faint not.
	Hoping for the future success of the EX-
PONENT, I remain yours in the cause of truth.
				MARY ELLEN MCKAY.


SOJOURNER TRUTH, who is now one hundred
and six years old, on receiving recently a pre-
sent of a silk dress from England, expressed a
hope that she might continue a sojourner here
below long enough to wear it out.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SCENES IN NAUVOO.
				-----
	   BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.
				-----
	A great change had been wrought during
the time that the Twelve had been absent from
Nauvoo--My father writing from there in July
1841 described the appearance of the city at
that time--He says, "You know there were
not more than thirty buildings in the city when
we left about two years ago; but at this time
there are about 1200, and hundreds of others
in progress, which will be finished soon.  On
Friday last seventy Saint came to Nauvoo, led
by Lorenzo Barnes, from Chester County, Penn-
sylvania, in wagons, living by the way. On
the next day a company came in wagons, from
Canada, all in good spirits, and in two or three
days after, they all obtained places to live in.
They are coming in from all parts of the vast
continent daily and hourly, and the work is
spreading in all of this land and calls for preach-
ing in all parts.  You will recollect when
we built our houses in the woods there was not
a house within a half a mile of us, now the
place, wild as it was at that time, as converted
into a thickly populated village."
	The following account which was printed in
a St. Louis paper will show the thriving con-
dition of the city in 1842. "The population of
Nauvoo is between 8000 and 9000, and of course
the largest town in the State of Illinois. How
long the Latter-day Saints will hold together
and exhibit their present aspect it is not for us
to say.  At this moment they present the ap-
pearance of an enterprising, industrious, sober
and thrifty population such  a population  in-
deed, as in the respects just mentioned, have no
rivals east, and we rather guess not even west
of the Mississippi."  Before the close of 1842
the population numbered about 15,000 and
scarcely three years had elapsed since the Saints
came there so nearly destitute of every worldly
thing, and by their industry in spite of sickness
and the many obstacles which they had to con-
tend with, they had accomplished more than
any  other community had done in the same
amount of time, but notwithstanding all these
signs of industry, temperance and virtue the
same spirit of jelously and persecution which
had been manifested towards them in Ohio, and
Missouri was imbibed by a portion of the people
of Illinois, and many false reports were cir-
culated by them to injure the Saints, but often
they had the opposite effect and enlisted the
sympathies of many honorable people, and occa-
sionally persons who visited our city would be
so agreeably surprised that they would publish
accounts which contradicted the false reports.
The following one which was written by a
Methodist Minister who visited Nauvoo in the
Spring of 1843 gives such a fair description of
our city and people as they were, that I would
like to see it reprinted for the benefit of those
who listen to all the rumors that are started
about the "Mormons" and believe that they
have sunk so much lower in the mire of cor-
ruption, since coming out to Utah--He says--
"At length the city burst upon my sight and
how sadly was I disappointed; instead of seeing
a few miserable log cabins and mud houses
which I expected to find, I was surprised to see
one of the most romantic places that I had
visited in the west.  The buildings, though
many of them were small and of wood, yet bore
the marks of neatness, which I have not seen
equalled in this country.  The far spread plain
at the bottom of the hill was dotted over with
the habitations, of men with such majestic pro-
fusion, that I was almost willing to believe my-
self mistaken; and instead of being in Nauvoo, of
Illinois, among Mormon that I was in Italy at
the city of Leghorn (which the location of
Nauvoo resembles very much) and among ec-
centric Italians, I gazed for some time with
fond admiration upon the plains below.  Here
and there arose a tall majestic brick house,
speaking loudly of the genius, and untiring
labors of the inhabitants, who have snatched
the place from the clutches of obscurity, and
wrested it from the bonds of disease, and in two
or three short years rescued it from a dreary
waste to transform it into one of the first cities
of the west.  The hill upon which I stood was
covered over with the dwellings of men, and
amid them was seen to rise the hewn stone and
already accomplished work of the temple, which
is now raised 15 or 20 ft. above the level of the
ground.  The few trees that were permitted to
stand were now in full foliage, and were scat-
tered with a sort of fantastic irregularity over
the slope of the hill."  This grove that he
mentioned was where the Saints met upon the
Sabbath day to worship or to hold their public
meetings.  He continues--"I passed on into
the more active parts of the city, looking into
every street and lane to observe all that was
passing.  I found all the people engaged in
some useful and healthy employment.  The
place was alive with business, much more so
than any place I have visited since the hard
times commenced.  I sought in vain for any-
thing that bore the mark of immorality; but
was both astonished and highly pleased at my
ill success.  I could see no loungers about the
streets, nor any drunkards, about the taverns.
I did not meet with those distorted features of
ruffians, or with the ill-bred or impudent, I
heard not an oath in the place, I saw not a
gloomy countenance; all were peaceful, polite,
and industrious.  I conversed with many lead-
ing men, and found them social and well-in-
formed, hospitable and generous.  I saw noth-
ing but order and I regulation in the society.
Where then, I exclaimed, is all this startling
proof of the utter profligacy of Nauvoo?  Where
in the name of God, is the immorality charged
upon the citizens of it; and what dreadful out-
breaking crimes have given men the license
to depreciate this place as much as they do?
Where is the gang of marauders, horse theives
and ruffians, the drunkards and vicious men of
Nauvoo?  Where are the horrid forms of human
beings distorted with hellish rage and maddened
ire?  Where are the dark and diabolical sup-
erstitions?  Where are those specimens of cre-
dulity and ignorance?  Where are those damn-
ing doctrines of demons?  Where in fine is
this slough, this sink of iniquity of which I
have heard so much?  Surely not in Nauvoo!
They must have got the wrong place, or wil-
fully lied about it.  I could but blush with
disappointed shame for my friends who had so
misinformed me, and very soon made up my
mind, like the Queen of Sheba, not to believe
any reports of enemies but to always like her,
go and see for myself."  Here is the testimony
of another gentile who saw it at that time. In
a letter he says, "Look and see what they have
done at Nauvoo, during the comparative
short time they have been there.  If they are
enabled to proceed as they have commenced
their town ere long will become a mighty city.
I do not believe there is another people in ex-
istence who could have made such improvement
in the same length of time under the same cir-
cumstances."
	I wish there were more honorable men, who
when they come among us could see one side
as well as the other, and had sufficient moral
courage when they go away to publish the truth
about the "Mormons!" As far as ourselves are
concerned it matters very little what our en-
emies think or say of us for we expect nothing
better of them and we know that in the end
they will be the sufferers.  I think that we
have shown to the world that we could live and
thrive upon a desert land which they had never
dreamed of inhabiting as well as subdue and
dwell in peace with the savage without any of
their assistance; but we know there are many
honest souls who have not yet heard this Gospel
preached and if the servants of God who have
enjoyed such great blessings and manifestations
of His power should refuse to obey his man-
dates to go forth and warn the inhabitants of
the earth of the judgments which are right at
their doors, that they may repent &c., and
gather up to these mountains where He has
promised to protect his people, they would be
under condemnation and could not escape the
wrath of the Almighty.  The Latter-day Saints
when they receive this Gospel understandingly
are prepared for trials and to make sacrifices
which thousands of them have done in leaving
their homes and native lands, their friends and
kindred ties that had to be broken, and many
have sacrifized worldly riches and what was
dearer than all their good name.  Oh how little
the outside world know or can understand the
motive that prompts the Latter-day Saints to
gather from the different countries and nations
to this far off land, among a people whom all
manner of evil is spoken of, but they knew the
voice of the true shepherd who has said "Ye
shall be hated of all men for my name's sake:
but he that endureth to the end shall be saved;
He has also said to his servants "Behold I send
you forth as sheep in the midst of wolves: be
ye therefore wise as serpents and harmless as
doves.  But beware of men: for they will de-
liver you up to the counsels and they will scourge
you in their synagogues and ye shall be brought
before the governers and kings for my sake for
a testimony against them and the Gentiles.
Fear not them which kill the body, but rather
fear him who is able to destroy both soul and
body."  The world may prate about the ignor-
ant Mormons but the lowliest among us and
even our little children understand the Scrip-
tures and plan of salvation better than they with
all their worldly wisdom.
			To be continued.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	Shall we not rejoice even in affliction? Do
we not see the mighty hand of God stretched
forth every day in our behalf? See the glorious
fields of grain, the sweet-smelling hay, the gar-
dens, the fruits; the flowers, all proclaim ye are
God's people! I have gathered, you from the
four quarters of the earth, and I will bless you!
I will pour forth into your laps the rich treas-
ures of the earth for ye are mine! Oh, be faith-
ful ye handmaidens of the God of heaven for
ye are indeed the blest wives and daughters of
the noble sons of Ephraim, who are ever seeking
for the welfare of Zion! let us help bear the
banners of mercy truth and justice in all meek-
ness, in all sincerity, let us do and dare in our
sphere of action: strive to bring forth a noble
race of sons and daughters, who will acknow-
ledge the hand of God, who will be more dili-
gent than we are in serving the Lord our God,
for they are heirs of truth, and will be lovers
of every good. They will go on conquering
and to conquer every influence that is not of
God, if we will live near unto Him who ruleth
the Universe. Then let us rejoice for we have
a glorious future before us, upward and ouward
be our motto! side by side with our husbands,
be our watchword! That we may receive the
blessed assureance that will be given to all who
strive to serve God and keep His command-
ments—"Well done good and faithful servants
enter thou into the glory of thy Lord."
	B. M.


THE American Woman Suffrage Association
will hold its next annual meeting in Louisville
Kentucky, Oct. 25 and 26. This will be very
soon after the Woman's Congress in Buffalo.
The Western and Eastern ladies who go to the
Congress in Buffalo will be able to attend the
Convention in Louisville on their return with-
out extra expense.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


"WAITING FOR MOTHER.

The old man sits in his easy chair
	Slumbering the moments away,
Dreaming a dream that is all his own,
	On this gladsome, peaceful day.
His children have gathered from far and near,
	His children's children beside—
And merry voices are echoing through
	The "Homestead's" halls so wide.

But far away, in the years long flown,
	Grandfather lives again:
And his heart forgets that it ever knew
	A shadow of grief or pain, 
For he sees his wife as he saw her then—
	A matron comely and fair,
With her children gathered around his board
	And never a vacant chair.

Oh! happy this dream of the "Anld Lang Syne,"
	Of the years long slipped away!
And the old man's lips have gathered a smile
	And his heart grows young and gay.
But a kiss falls gently upon his brow,
	From his daughter's lips so true:
"Dinner is ready; and, father, dear,
	We are only waiting for you."

The old man wakes at his daughter's call,
	And looks at the table near—
"There's one of us missing, my child," he says,
	"We will wait till mother is here."
There are tears in the eyes of his children, then,
	As they gaze on an empty chair:
For many a lonely year has passed
	Since "mother" sat with them there.

But the old man pleads still wilfully;
	"We must wait for mother, you know!"
And they let him rest in his old arm-chair
	Till the sun at last sinks low.
Then, leaving a smile for the children here,
	He turns from the earth away,
And has gone to "mother," beyond the skies,
	With the close of the quiet day.

	MARY D. BRIND.
Selected,


[Column 1]

PEN SKETCH OF AN ILLUSTRIOUS
WOMAN.

ELIZA R. SNOW SMITH.

CONTINUED.
	Sister Eliza although intimately associated in
the family relation with Pres. Brigham Young
and having resided under the same roof more
than a quarter of a century, and assisted in
various ways to promote his domestic happiness
did not as many would have done, sit down and
spend her time in 'mourning' his death. She
was too much interested in the work in which
he had passed his lifetime; and seemed to renew
her diligence, if that were possible, in promoting
the growth of all the organizations of women
which existed in the Church.
	During that fall Sister Eliza R. S. S. visited
many of the settlements in Salt Lake and other
counties attended meetings and encouraged the
sisters to increase their efforts to do good, and 
help roll on the Latter-day work.
	On the 12th of Dec. a meeting was held to
discuss the best method to obtain signatures to
a petition for a 16th amendment to Congress,
the women of Utah of which she was the leading
spirit, haveing been solicited to aid the National
Woman's Suffrage Association in petitioning.
Sister Eliza suggested a special meeting to be
held Dec. 22nd. At this special meeting an
organization was effected of the Relief Society
of this Stake. Sister Eliza moving in the mat-
ter as President elect of women's organizations,
and making the nominations according to a
precedent established by Brigham Young at a
meeting held in Ogden City July 19, a short
time previous to his decease. This was a new
movement, but in strict harmony with the order
of the organizations and by the permission of the
presiding authorities of the priesthood, and
nothing could be more systematic or better cal-

[Column 2]

culated to further the growth of these institu-
tions than this method of associating together
and reporting in a conference. After this part of
the programme had been carried out, then the
arrangement was made to canvass the Territory
for signatures to the 16th amendment petition
to Congress. In this labor Sister Eliza assisted
not only by advice but actual work in writing
out petitions, receiving, counting names and
preparing the rolls to be forwarded to Washing-
ton. It was at the Convention held early in
January 1878, just following, that the ladies of
the N. W. S. A., spoke so emphatically in de-
fense of Mormon women, and by this work of
petitioning, these same ladies were enabled to
comprehend what power for good was in the
hands of Mormon women.
	It was about this time that the Deseret Silk
Association was making some rapid strides and
Sister Eliza was one of the most earnest workers
in the silk enterprise.
	Speaking of definite public work of organiz-
ing done by Sister E. R. S. Smith, much might
be said and written; however we can only men-
tion in a general way a part of what she has
really been instrumental in doing. Early in the
year 1878 having previously given the store in-
to other hands she commenced with renewed
energy traveling and holding meetings among
the different Stakes of Zion, and assisted in
perfecting the Relief Society organizations after
the same order as in Salt Lake Stake. May 2d
at a Relief Society Conference in Ogden, Apos-
tle Erastus Snow being present also D, H.
Peery and other local authorities of that Stake
which had only been partially effected, was
completed by electing a full board of officers.
	From Ogden Sister Eliza proceeded north
through Box Elder Co., and Malad Valley,
visiting the various towns and settlements or-
ganizing etc., and returning by way of Weber
Co. held two or three meetings in that Stake; in
all holding over thirty meetings in fifteen days.
In July of the same year she traveled through
Utah and Wasatch and in August through
Sanpete, Cache, Morgan and Summit Stakes;
performing as much missionary labor as it was
possible for any one to do; for everywhere she
spoke earnestly and prayed fervently and
words of counsel and instruction such as seemed
suited to the temporal and spiritual condition
of the sisters young and old, flowed from her
lips. Sister Eliza traveled hundreds of miles
during that summer in all kinds of weather, not
in an easy carriage waited upon by servants, but
as opportunity afforded in all sorts of convey-
ances furnished by the people among whom
her lot was cast, and it is scarcely possible to
say how many times she spoke in public. It
was in some respects an eventful year for Sister
Eliza and for Zion, for during that summer
while attending a meeting at Farmington the
idea of an organization for children, such as
now exists was suggested to her mind by some
remards make by Mrs. Aurelia Spencer Rogers.
This hint has since developed into something
tangible known as the Primary Associations,
due to the active, earnest, efficient and one
might almost say indefatigable labors of this
remarkable woman and her co-workers in these
enterprises for the good of Zion. These Asso-
ciations are in our opinion an important factor
in the kingdom of God upon the earth, for they
lie at the very foundation of the Hope of
Israel.
	The first organization was formed on the 11th,
of August 1878 in Farmington and the first
meeting held on the 25th inst. but as it was
not very successful the Association date their
organizations from Sept. 7, 1878. About the
same time some Associations were organized in
Salt Lake City the 11th Ward taking the
lead.
	To be continued.


THE PRIMARIES.

	EDITOR EXPONENT: Permit me through
your columns to say a few words that have been
suggested by visting the Primary Association.
I love to go there, I sometimes think that the
mothers do not appreciate the privileges their
children enjoy; if they did I think they could
spare an hour once in a while to encourage
them by their presence. I often think that I
would like to have our friends in the States
visit these little meetings, I think they would be
forced to take back what has been said in re-
gard to our children; the offspring of plural
marriage being the poor imbeciles, etc., which
they have been represented.
 At the last meeting I attended the President
called upon a little girl to preside, and I can
truly say that is surpassed any thing I had
ever seen in a child. She went through with
the programme with dignity; and then in a 
very pleasant manner said to the visiting sisters
"this fills our programme and we would like
you to talk to us," addressing herself to one who
was present. That programme was indeed
beautiful. Little ones lisping the Lord's prayer
and reading from the Old and New Testament
essays and dialogues on prayer and many other
good things that I cannot remember, and last
but not least the singing from their little Hymn
Books, it seemed like angels voices singing;
praises to our Father.
 John Quincy Adams, President of the United
States once said, when he was an old man he
loved to say the prayer that he learned at his
mother's knee,and do not think that these little
boys and girls will not remember their little meet
ings and the good things they have learned there,
and their President and her counselors that are
laboring hard to prepare them for the great
and glorious future that awaits them if faith-
ful.
 And Sister E. R. Snow Smith, they under-
stand that she was the first organizer and when
told that she will visit them their countenances
beam with joy and happiness, and everywhere
one goes it is the same; how beautiful is such
love and truly she is blessed among women; for,
she has not only the love and good will of every
Latter-day Saint but she has the prayers of the
little children ascending to our Father in her
behalf and Jesus has said "of such is the king-
dom of heaven." I rejoice in Woman's work
and that the restitution has commenced, and
that if we are faithful and seek unto our Father
for his divine aid and assistance, we shall gain
step by step, what we have lost and stand by
the side of our brother man, a wise counselor
and friend in helping to spread light and truth,
and in the redemption of the human race; and
we will take care of the babies too, but we will
pray to our Father for wisdom and ability to
assist in his work, but I must close, or I shall
say something on the Woman question, and it
is thought, by some, that that, had better be
laid on the shelf but it will not be fore our Father
has decreed it otherwise.		R. A. W.


MRS. Dominis, sister of King Kalakua of
the Sandwich Islands is acting as Regent while
the king is away.  Her brother has chosen her
as his successor to the throne.  Mrs. Margaret
Mitcheel and other "Mormon" ladies have had
the pleasure of meeting Mrs. Dominis while on
the Sandwich Islands and speak of her as a
most etimable lady, very kind in her manner
as well as very agreeable and attractive.
 A NEW book "Harvest Gleanings," by Miss
Anna Gardiner, is now in the hands of the
publishers, The introduction is by Rev. Phebe
A. Hanaford, and the book is dedicated to
Mrs. Charlotte A. Joy. These ladies are all
engaged in reform work.  We have no doubt
the book will prove a useful and instructive
one and a help to the advocates of Woman's
Suffrage and other kindred subjects.

SALT LAKE CITY  SEPTEMBER 1, 1881.


WAITING,
	PRESIDENT GARFIELD, so long a severe suf-
ferer, and seemingly near his appointed time for
release from suffering, still lingers on the border
of the shaowy land. The physicians at the
present express considerable hope, and Mrs. Gar-
field, a firm faith in his recovery, a con-
viction that in his case a "a miracle will
be wrought." The sympathies of
the nation are with her in this trying
hour, and it must be a source of satisfaction in
the midst of such a fiery ordeal to know that all
the people are interested and thousands sending
up petitions to Him who rules and overrules and
holds the destiny of mations in His hands, that
He will sare the life of this great man, the
Chief Executive of the American Republic. Mrs.
Garfield's courage and fortitude in the midst of
her earnest solicitude for the welfare of her hus-
band is another proof of woman's strength of
endurance under more than ordinary affliction.
A few more days at least will determine the
important question, whether President Garfield
has vitality sufficient to rally after so great a
struggle with the grim monster death. Mean-
time, the nation is waiting in anxious and pray-
erful suspense, for that the people loved him is
evident on every hand.


LAY UP SOME GRAIN.
	In consideration of the bountiful harvest of
Wheat and other grain which the people in this
valley are blest with, we would suggest that the
sisters recall to mind the request made of them
by President Brigham Young in the fall of 1876,
through the columns of the WOMAN'S EXPO-
NENT, and put in practice the sound advice then
given, To be sure there has been a commenda-
ble effort made to save some grain each year, and
the sisters have not been unmindful of the
counsel they recieved, but in this season of
plenty there might be almost as much done as in
all the preceding years, the prosperity seems to
be so much greater.
  If this golden opportunity goes by, and the
people become careless and indifferent to coun-
sel, because of the great abundance which the
Lord has given, it might be a source of sorrow
in days to come.  Be wise in time and lay by a
portion of the grain with which your labors have
been crowned.  Do not think it of less conse-
quence because of its abundance.  This counsel
was not given for one season, or two, but in or-
der that there might be something reserved
against a time of scarcity, a time of famine,
which has been prophesied of by ancient and 
modern prophets, and will be sure to come soon-
er or later, notwithstanding that at the present
time there seems to be no lack of any good
thing.


The trees are loaded with fruit till they bend
beneath the heavy burden. Vegetation is more
abundant in all its forms than usual. Even the
flowers have been more luxuriant and full, and
fragrance sweeter in proportion. Never was
there a lovelier summer than the present one;
Nature has worn her most royal robes of beauty,
and hill, dale, grove and woodland have rejoiced
the heart, and cheered and gladdened the eye of
man.
	The sisters in their various organizations have,
from time to time, been accumulating in their
bins and storehouses, little by little, and it has
seemed up hill work, and sometimes their cour-
age almost failed. In this plentiful season per-
haps much larger quantities may be gathered
up and preserved. Everything seems to suggest
the approach of great changes in nations and
governments and in view of this, and the fulfil-
ment of the predictions of seers and wise men,
it is well to "lay up something against a rainy
day," a day of less prosperity. Bread is more
precious and desirable than gold, or silver, or
gems. It is the duty of every wife, of every
mother to use her influence to promote agricul-
ture, for unless the earth is tilled and its pro-
ducts cultivated, of what benefit would be all
the gold of Golconda, Utah, Nevada, or all the
precious gems of the whole earth? Then let
us encourage the dignity of the farmer, the gar-
dener and all who follow the profession of out
Father Adam, and maintain the dignity of
Ruth, who was not ashamed to go and glean.
Then, perchance, there may be some land-
owners liberal enough to leave a few sheaves
for the pretty gleaners in the harvest field.


SECRETARIES Y. L. M. I. A.

	The Stake Secretaries of Y. L. M. I. A. are
earnestly requested to send in complete reports
(which will comprise each branch) to the Secre-
tary of the Central Board, as early as the middle
of September, that an accurate report of all the
organizations may be presented to the October
Conference.  This has never yet been done, be-
cause Stake Secretaries have failed to send in
proper reports.  Those who have reported in full
from date of organization need only send from
date of former statistics.  It is an important
matter and should be promptly attended to.
The Secretary of the Central Board is expected
to hand in the full report two or three days pre-
vious to the October Conference.  Bear Lake
Stake, Box Elder and Weber sent very credia-
ble reports last year, but there were not any
from several other localities, so it would have
been very unsatisfactory to attempt a complete
report, and was therefore laid over to the pres-
ent year.  Be sure and state number of branches
in the Stake, as well as number of members, and
all other items worthy of mention,  not forget-
ting the editing of manuscript papers. Direct all
reports to the Secretary, Louie Wells,  Office
WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City.

	THE Children’s Fair for Salt Lake County
will be held in the Social Hall in this City in
September, commencing Septemer 22d, and not
5th, as stated previously.  It will continue
three days, under the auspices of Mrs. Ellen C.
S. Clawson and her counselors and assistants.
Particulars will be given in the Deseret News
and the Exponent of September 15. Prices of
admission will be, adults 10 cents, children 5
cents.
	THE children of the Primary Assoclation of
West Jordan Ward have proven what can be
accomplished in  very short time by five cent
Temple donations. In three months their fund
amounted to six dollars and sixty cents, which
their president, Mrs. Eliza Bird, paid over to
Bishop Hunter on Thursday, August' 25th. It
is encouragin to see the little ones making such
noble efforts towards building up Zion, setting
an example worthy of imitation by many who
are negligent in these matters.
 THE semi-monthly meeting of the ladies in
the Fourteenth Ward, Assembly Rooms, Satur-
day. August 27th, was very interesting. Presi-
dent E. R. S. Smithr presided.  Opened by sing-
ing "We thank Thee, O God for a prophet;”
prayey by Counselor B. W. Smith; singing, "Let
Zion in her beauty rise.” Minutes were read
from the Y. L. M. I. A. of the Third Ward and
from two Primary Associations.  Sister Eliza
made some excellent remarks, which were very
gratifying to the sisters, and suggested those in
the audience should occupy the time. Many
testimonies were borne of the manifestation of
the healing power of God, and a goodly portion of
the Holy Spirit rested upon the congregation.
Towards the close of the meeting Counselors
B. W. Smith and Phebe Woodruff adressed the
meeting. Singing, ',O my Father,” etc.; prayer
by Counselor P. Woodruff. Meeting adjourned
for two weeks.

  A SELECT party of'the friends of Dr. Romania
B. Pratt, met at her residence on the evening of
August 8, ult., to celebrate the anniversary of
her birthday.  A few of her lady friends present-
ed her with a plain gold ring, as a slight token of
their sincere love and esteem, and some other
valuable presents.  A letter from one who was
unavoidably absent was read and its contents
accepted as the sentiment of those assembled.
The evening was passed in happy social con-
verse.
	Mrs. Pratt was the first woman graduate in
the medical profession among this people. At
a time and under circumstances which would
have seemed insurmountable to many, she left
her family of little children in the care of her
mother and went to Philadelphla, entered the
Woman’s Medical College, and graduated from
that institution with the title of M.D., which


she had faithfully earned, by her own energy
and perseverance.  She spent some time at the
Eye and Ear Infirmary, and at the Elmira
Water Cure Establishment, and a few months
at Bellevue Hospital in New York City. Mrs.
Pratt has a genuine love for the practice of med-
icine and surgery, and possesses within herself,
with the blessing of God upon her efforts,
all the possibilities of future success in
the profession she has chosen.  Though
small in stature she has already proven
herself a brave little woman and equal to the
emergency in some very difficult and complicat-
ed cases.  Utah may well be proud of her wo-
men doctors and in a Territory where women
have the ballot it is only proper to suppose that
they will make some proficiency in the higher
branches of education and advancement.
	On Wednesday afternoon, August 12th, in
company with Sisters E. R. S. Smith and E. C.
S. Cl-wson, we had the pleasure of attending a
meeting of the Primary Association in the 21st
Ward, Mrs. Mary Davis presiding.  Mrs. Emma
Tune and quite a number of the children from
the 20th Ward Primary Association were also
present, by invitation.  The weather was not
propitious, but notwithstanding the pouring rain
there was a goodly number assembled together.
Meeting opened by singing, "Children of the
Saints," prayer by Sister E. Gange; singing,
"Don't Kill the Birds."  The President made a
few remarks, and the minutes were read and
accepted.  The programme previously prepared
then began with a dialogue upon "Liquor Sa-
loons," by Eva Barfoot and Julia Webb; song, 
Robert Jones; recitation, Annie Broadbent;
song, Ella Olsen and Alice Sanders; recitation, 
Sarah Ann Rogers; recitation, Margaret Irish; 
flute duet, Joseph Smith and John Owen; reci-
tation, Herbert Folger; recitation, Emma Rog-
ers; duet, Bertha Bailiss and Kate Williams; 
recitation, Annie Irish; recitation, Amelia
Hanson; recitation, Emily Irish; song, Rose
Miller, "Little Acts of Kindness;" recitation, 
Elizabeth Nixon; organ and flute duet, Fewson 
Smith and John Miller. 
   The visitors then made brief remarks and
suggestions.  Sister Eliza referred to the book of 
questions and answers she was engaged in pre-
paring, that it would be a great help to the offi-
cers in imparting instruction.  Miss Emily
Hanson was nominated and sustained to act as
Counselor to the President of the Primary As-
sociation.  Mrs. Davis stated that they had
enrolled the names of 176 children, their average
attendance is about 75.  Meeting closed with
singing "We thank thee, O God, for a Prophet,"
prayer by Master Thomas Mulholland.  Ad-
journed for one week.  This association has only
been organized about three months, but is in a 
most prosperous condition.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 53


[Column 1]

OBITUARY.

	Died: in Salt Lake City, August the 6th,
1881, Sarah Beanland, wife of Joseph Bean, and
daughter of the late John and Elizabeth Bean-
land, of Springwood Place, Bolton, near Bradford,
Yorkshire, England.
	She was born September 1st, 1821, and shortly
after her husband received the Gospel as restored
in these latter days, she was baptized into the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, May
1st, 1843. She emigrated with her husband to
the United States in fulfillment of the divine
command "to gather out from among the na-
tions," in 1855, but could only proceed as far as
Cincinnati, for lack of means. Her determined
purpose in the great Latter-day work, and her
unswerving faith in the protecting care of the
Almighty were well exhibited in the fortitude
displayed by her on the voyage across the sea.

[Column 2]

Situated as emigrants were, in the steerage of
sailing vessels, under such unpropitions circum-
stances she gave birth to a daughter, her exu-
berant spirits never flagging, nor her rejoicings
ceasing in the faith and power of her holy relig-
ion.
	In 1859 she crossed the plains for Utah. On
account of the heavy loading of the ox teams
there were but few miles of the long journey
that she did not walk and carry her infant plod-
ding, day after day, through the burning sands
of the Black Hills, but never a murmur or com-
plaint was heard to pass her lips. When almost
exhausted herself she could be seen encouraging
others, of her wearied companions, in the good-
ness of God, and the bright prospects that awaited
them in glorious Zion. Arriving in Salt Lake
City, she with her husband and family, settled
in the 11th Ward, where she continued to reside
until her death. She filled the position of coun-
selor to Sister McRae, president of the Relief
Society, which position gave her ample oppor-
tunity for the exercise of those gifts so prominent
in her, being unbounded benevolence and solici-
tude for the welfare of others. Whatever the
vicissitudes of life, her uncomplaining nature
always wore the pleasing aspect of a contented
mind. She portrayed a meekness, and patience, 
and a devotedness to her husband and family,
her religion and her God, as becoming a Saint.
She was a faithful wife and mother unto death,
valiant in the testimony of Jesus, her hopes
never flagging nor her faith wavering in the
promises of eternal life. She was the mother of
eleven children and had nineteen grand child-
ren.  After an attack of cholera morbus, which
lasted two days, she succumbed to the power of
the destroyer. She retained her consciousness
up to the hour of her death, and passed away
as peacefully as in a gentle sleep, in the full as-
surance of a resurrection with the just, to reign
with Christ upon the earth.—Des. News.

	Sister Naomi R. daughter of David Lebaron
and Zuba King, and wife of James S. Holman,
deceased, after an illness of three days, departed
this life at 7 o'clock P. M., on Thursday the 11th,
inst at Holden Millard Co. Utah. She was born
in Leroy, Genesee Co. N. Y. Oct. 7th, 1816. Mar-
ried J. S. Holman, March 24th, 1833; joined the
Church in an early day and passed through all
the drivings and persecutions of those days; in
1836 received a patriarchal blessing under the
hands of Joseph Smith Sen., that has been a 
guide to her through her life, which has been a
very eventful one, and, but a few days before
her death she was heard to say that her life
seemed like a dream, when she thought of what
she had endured in her lifetime. She came to
Utah in 1848 with six small children, following
her husband who came the year previous;
	She was the mother of 12 children, her grand-
children numbering 52, and great grandchildren
16. She was a devoted wife and mother, a good
neighbor, and a faithful Latter-day Saint, and
has been for years an energetic member of the
Relief Society and was always willing to respond
to every call that was made of her. She passed
away peacefully surrounded by her relatives and
friends, and was laid away to await the resurec-
tion of the just.
	PAULINE LARSON.


AT REST.

	Another dear one has departed from this to a
bettter state of existence; our aged sister Patsy
Ann De Berry. She was familiarly known in
the 10th Ward, Salt Lake City, where she re-
sided as "Grandma Smith," having been sealed
to the late Samuel H. Smith, brother to the
Prophet Joseph Smith.  She was born February


4th, 1808, in Simpson County, Kentucky, and
was the youngest child of William Lowe and
Margaret Fare.  She was desirous of joining
herself to some church in her youthful days, but
her father desired her not to do so, as he said the
true church was not upon the earth, but that it
would be before long; he might not, he added, live
to see it, but she would, and become identified
with it.  She was baptized into the Church of
Jesus Chirst of Latter-day Saints, March 10th,
1835, by James Emmett.  She never gathered
with the Saints until the fall of 1875, owing to
her husband not having embraced the gospel,
she having lived with him until his death.  She
was well posted in all the affairs of the Church
in the mountains, as well as to a great extent
in the nations of the earth, as she took all the
publications of the Church as they were publish-
ed from time to time.  She lived the life of a 
true Latter-day Saint, full of faith and good
works, keeping very faithfully "the Word of
Wisdom."  I believe she never tasted tea, coffee
or intoxicants of any kind, not even home-made
wine.  She went to her rest at the ripe old age
of 73 years, 6 months and 9 days, on Saturday
afternoon, August 13th, 1881, at 2 p. m., with
the assurance of coming forth in the morning of 
the first resurrection.  "Peace to her ashes."
								T. C. J.


NAUVOO TEMPLE.

	Nauvoo, or "The beautiful," as it was some-
times designated, was formerly called Commerce
and sometimes the "City of Joseph." October
3rd, 1840, at a general conference it was resolved
to build a "House of the Lord" there, to com-
mence it within ten days of that date and to ap-
propriate every tenth day to the building of the
sacred edifice. April 6th, 1841, the first stone
was laid amidst the most straightened circum-
stances of the Saints. In the Spring of 1843, the
Temple was raised fifteen or twenty feet above
the level of the ground. October 6th, 1845, it
was so far finished as to allow a General Confer-
ence of the Church to be held in it. At this
conference the subject of the evacuation of
Nauvoo was canvassed before the Saints and
committees for the sale of houses, farms, lots,
etc., appointed. Sunday, the day before, 5,000
saints met in it for the first time. May 1st,
1846, public services were held in the Temple,
the amount said to have been expended on it by
the Saints exceeds $1,000,000. November 10th,
1848, it was fired by an incendiary, the light of
its fire was visible for 30 miles. May 27th, 1850,
a tornado blew down the north wall and so
shook the building that the Icarians who had
been engaged in rebuilding the edifice for their
use deemed it advisable to pull down the east
and south walls leaving only the west wall.
									A. F.


A PLEASANT SURPRISE.

			MILL CREEK, August 5th, 1881.
	Quite a number of relatives and friends, of
Sister Jeanette Park, assembled at her residence
in Mill Creek Ward, July the 20th to welcome
her home after a three months visit South.  At
about 5 o'clock P.M., the party assembled await-
ing the arrival of the Southern train, which was
to bring Sister Park home.  When it did arrive
her surprise and joy at meeting so many friends,
can better be imagined than expressed.  At 6
o'clock the party sat down to tables sumptuously
loaded, and partook of a repast that was elegant
to the eye as well as delicious to the taste.  There
were about 100 persons present.  Sister Park re-
ceived some very useful and elegant presents
well worthy of mention.  After supper was over
various amusements were engaged in; such as
music, dancing, singing etc., which lasted until
about 12 o'clock at night, when the happy as-
semblage dispersed, feeling they had enjoyed a
very happy time.


Sister Park likes the Southern country very
much, and while there she had a very nice time,
especially working in the St. George Temple for
her dead, and she feels to bear her testimony of
the Holy Spirit that surrounds the Temple, caus-
ing such a peaceful good feeling to prevail in and
around the Temple, and throughout the place.
							O. S.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
		REPORTS.

	The ladies regular semi-monthly meetings in
the 14th ward in this city are still continued
although of late we have omitted publishing on
account of so many conference minutes from
different parts of the Territory; but as these
meetings are of special consequence we deem it
wise to make notices now and then for the
benefit of those who have not the privilege of
attending.  These meetings may be compared
to the high school department of womens' or-
ganizations.  
	Saturday July 30. Counselor Sarah M. 
Kimball presided in the absence of the Presid-
ent M. Isabella Horne.  After the usual exer-
cises, minutes of the previous meeting were read
and accepted, also minutes of the 12th, and 11th, 
ward Primary Associations.  
	Mrs. S. M. Kimball was the first speaker, she
referred to this as the vacation season, and
thought the physical condition affected the
spiritual somewhat.  Mentioned that Sister
Eliza R. S. S., who had been so long away was
present also Sister Horrocks who had just re-
turned from St. George.  
	Sister E. R. S. Smith, said she had come more
particularly to electioneer, that it was an im-
portant point; she had been very little with her
sisters in the city for eight months, but realized
how beautiful was the love of sisterhood, es-
pecially when the tie is the Gospel.  Our Father
has appointed for our growth and advancement
our meeting together.  "These said the Savior
"will I make my jewels."  These blessings
which it is our privilege to obtain how impor-
tant.  What consequences depend upon our
course!  Everything else in comparison with
eternal riches is as nothing.  In arrang-
ing questions and answers for the children, 
when I find that only two were permitted to
enter the land of Canaan--it seems strange. 
But we find that these two wholly honored God.  
Even Moses took honor to himself.  When 
thirsting and God gave him the rod and told
him what to do, he spoke to the children of
Israel as though it was of his own power the
miracle was performed, the Lord showed that
He would be obeyed.  I feel that every one
present is a follower of the Lord.  It requires
great strength to do the will of God. Whether 
attending to home duties or as minute women
called to perform some extra service, we can
little by little, bring every power into the service
of God.  Cannot God avert the hand of the
destroyer?  We can feel affliction but cannot so
plainly see the wisdom thereof.  The little ones
are being gathered home.  Our Father has
said that in the last days He would lay up
much precious seed for Himself.  Let us my 
sisters try to draw a little nearer to God, hold
a little nearer communion with him.  I think
we can to a considerable extend mould ourselves
to his purposes.  The Spirit of God will not
approve of excessive grief.  The virtue lies in
overcoming grief.  Who has the same means
to assist them in overcoming the sorrow of death
that the Saints of God have.  I have wondered 
how those who have not the knowledge that we
have can bear up.  But there are worse ills
than death.  To turn away from the faith.  
	Remember to-morrow is election day.  It is
our duty to vote, sisters let no trifling thing
keep you at home.  So many brethren are
away, that every sister should be prompt, and
help sustain those in office that will sustain the
kingdom of God.  
	Sister C. Horrocks, spoke of her recant visit
to St. George. and that the remembrance of it
and her visit to the Temple almost overcome
her; said she felt to try and live to the teach-
ings given there, and here in the sisters meet-
ings.  
	Dr. Romania B. Pratt, felt it was good to
attend the meetings for she always had her
spirit fed and strengthened there.  It is a 
great privilege that we can go to the polls and
vote and elect those men who are best for such
trustworthy positions, It is the very end that
the most talented women of the world are
striving for.  It is particularly important in
Utah that we be careful who presides over us.
We are getting so comfortable that we are also
becoming careless when we find ourselves sur-
rounded and overcome; we will begin to call on
the Lord to deliver us.  But it is our duty to
help ourselves.  Every woman who has the
franchise should use it.  Sisters your individual
influence may be a lever in the community.
We are entitled to maintain honest men in posi-
tion.  We have the right so to do.  Let us
think what would be our fate were we once to 
lose power.  There is a set determination to
overcome this people.  If we would not have
past injustices repeated, we must look to our-
selves. 
	Dr. E. B. Ferguson. Spoke very eloquently
upon the suffrage question said she had been in-
terested in and engaged in working for suffrage
twenty years; when she came to Utah and
learned with what wisdom women used the
ballot; she wished those in the East could under-
stand the benefit. When the children of Israel 
departed from the teachings given them they
suffered in consequence.  If we prove negligent
the Lord may suffer us to become in bondage
to those who would oppress us.  Outside men
are elected by party rather than by the people.  
	Mrs. S. M. Kimball:  said she had intended
to go to the Primary Meetings but was hindered
by a caller she had since wished she had invited
him to go with her; felt we were under obliga-
tions to give attention to public matters.  
"Every privilege becomes a duty."
	"Mrs. B. W. Smmith thought all citizens
should go to the polls and vote the right ticket.  
If we sisters elect our party we may receive some 
favors we desire;  if our enemies are elected we
may be disfranchised.  Sisters use your influ-
ence with others for good.  
	Mrs. P. L. Kimball:  It is of vital importance
to us as a people that we hold the reins of gov-
ernment in our own hands as much as possible.  
Deduct the womens' vote and see what might
follow.  Leave your work on Monday morning
until you have voted, and be sure you have
the right ticket.  I have never missed voting
since the ballot was given to the women of Utah.  
Meeting adjourned for two weeks singing dox-
ology.  Benediction by Mrs. May A. Freeze.  
	14th Ward Saturday Aug. 13.   Mrs. M. I. 
Horne presiding.  After singing and prayer
minutes were read from 11th, 12th, 18th, 10th, 
ward Primary Associations.  Sister Horne
made some remarks upon principle and recom-
mended the sisters to use herbs and faith in-
stead of employing outside physicians, thought
faith and works should go together.  I believe in
our women employing midwives instead of male 
physicians, those that have the inspiration of
God many times to preserve, as Moses was saved 
in his day.  Those who are called to teach
should speak of these things and of existing
evils.  Doctors often extort from the poor un-
justifiably, such things should be remedied.  If
you employ a Doctor adhere to their direction.  
Dr. Ferguson has said that sulphur and cream
of tartar given twice a week to children would
prevent throat diseases.  The revelations given
by Joseph Smith are more particularly adapted 
to us.  We are to be tried, there is no excel-
ency attained without exertion.  Let us seek 
to perfect ourselves before the Lord.  
	Mrs. B. W. Smith, said those that attended
these meetings are the wheat; those that watch
and pray and try to set a good example and
try to live the religion of Christ.  We do not
exercise as much faith as we shouold.  The Lord
is able to do all things for our good and the
good of our children.  I hope the sisters who 
go out to represent us in the world may be 
belssed.  
	Mrs. E. S. Taylor, spoke of her visit to Mor-
gan Co., they had live institutions there, more
active than in Salt Lake, we ought to be more
appreciative because we have greater advan-
tages than the women of the world.   Why have
any of the Saints lost their former faith.  I
fear there is a wrong in some in placing too
much faith in physicians, who do not under-
stand as much as they profess.  God should be
our great physican.  
	Mrs H. C. Young, said we do not realize as
we should the responsibility that we bear be-
fore God to the people; we should enter the
house of God with pure minds.  Spoke upon
plural narriage and tithing, the organization of
the human system how supported etc. 
	Mrs. M. P. Young and Mrs. M. I. Horne
made some suggestive remarks and the meet-
ing was adjourned for two weeks with singing
and prayer by Mrs. Julia Howe.  

	ST. DAVID CACHISE CO. ARIZONA

EEITOR EXPONENT:  
	We send you a short account of our progres-
sion in this far away place, we held our third
Annual Meeting May 4th, 1881, our account
being read showed that we had accumulated
during the year $51.50; disbursed to missionar-
ies 10.50;  school house 9.25.  For quilt mat-
erial and other things 24.40.  Balance in the 
Tretsury 7.35.  Meetings held during the year
24; members, 15; average attendance 9. 
	We are trying to do all the good we can, our
President is alive to her duty and feels a great
desire for the advancement of the Relief Society
but it seems as though we have not much chance 
here at present.  Our settlement is growing in
numbers and we hope we shall be joined soon
by some good faithful sisters that can and will
assist us in our Society.  We have a very good
day school and Sunday School, and a Primary
Association so that our children ought to ad-
vance, we feel very thankful for the privileges, 
that we are enjoying at the present time in re-
gard to our children for they have had very
little chance for education, since we came here
over four years ago.  We are always glad to
receive the EXPONENT and wish there were more 
here that felt as though they could subscribe
for it.  Praying God to bless, and aid you in
your labors, we remain your sisters in the
Gospel.  
			CYRINA MERRILL.  Pres. 
			MARIA MCRAE, Sec'y.


AUCKLAND NEW ZEALAND

	EDITOR EXPONENT.—This branch of the
Relief Society was organized by Elder George
Batt, Dec. 22, 1880. Sister Isabel Lucy ap-
pointed President, and Sister Day and Fox her
Counselors, Sister Williams, Secretary, Sister
Miles Treasurer. We commenced to accumu-
late a fund which was cheerfully subscribed to
by most of the sisters, some giving money,
others articles of clothing, etc., un[-]il at the date
of writing, our books show[-] the sum of £10.14,0
as having been received, and disbursed, £105,0
to the poor, and remainder to assist the mis-
sionaries in their labors in this land; we feel to
thank God that we are considered worthy to
assist in the great work of redemption.
	In June 9, we bade farewell to our beloved
President and her counselors, viz., Sisters Lucy.


and Day, they leaving this place for the land
of Zion, whereupon Sister Hannah Cox was
called to preside and was sustained by unani-
mous vote of the Society, which now numbers
15 members, we are anxious to do our duty,
and are assisted in this at times by Elder
Romley meeting with and occasionally instruct-
ing us, in relation to the same; his motto is "if
we do but little, let us do that little well," which
motto we adopt and hope to be able to carry
out. The Society meets semi-monthly—once for
sewing and once for business purposes, we read
with pleasure in the EXPONENT of the efforts
of our sisters at home in Zion to assist in rolling
on the work of God, and anxiously look for-
ward to the time when we will be privileged to
be with you in person as we now are in spirit,
for with the poet we truly say,

"I know it is the promised land;
My home, my home is there."

	That God may bless you in your efforts to do
good and all who favor Zion and her interests,
is the prayer of your sisters in the Gospel of
Christ.
				HANNAH COX, Pres't.
				SARAH WILLIAMS, Sec'y.

WASATCH STAKE.

	Meeting of Relief Society of Wasatch Stake
held in Heber City Hall August 5th 1881.
Present on the stand, President Emma Brown
and Counselor Mary Daybell, the presidents
of the various Societies and Associations of the
Stake Pres. H[--] Bishops Foreman and
Murdock and other brethren. Meeting called
to order by Pres. Brown, singing, prayer by
Sister Duke, singing, "Spirit of God like a fire
is burning." Minutes of Conference read and
approved. Report read by Sec'y. The Pres.
of the several Societies gave verbal reports all
seeming to feel well. Presidents of Primary
Associations gave very favorable reports. The
children are striving to improve; Sister Brown
made some appropriate remarks. Adjourned
till 2 o'clock P. M.
	Afternoon session opened by usual exercises,
prayer by Sister Cluff. As the Sec. of the Y.
L. M. I. A., was not present one was chosen.
The Presidents of the Y. L. Associations gave
in their reports all seemed to feel well and im-
proving in good works. Sister Duke, from
Provo City, addressed the conference a short
time gave good instructions to mothers with re-
gard to training and instructing their children.
Spoke on Celestial Marriage, said it was a re-
vealed law from heaven, to the Prophet Joseph;
the speaker had heard the Prophet speak on
Celestial Marriage. Exhorted the sisters to be
faithful in keeping all the commandments of
God our Father leaving nothing undone that
might insure our salvation and exaltation in
the kingdom of Heaven. Sister Moulton gave
some very excellent instructions, Bishops
Foreman and Murdock gave good counsel said
mothers should be very watchful of their
daughters that they should not keep company
with outsiders and thereby be led astray.
Pres. Hatch was very much in earnest telling
the sisters to see after their daughters that
they are at home at night; and also to know
what company they are keeping. It is the duty
of mothers to look after their children. Sister
Brown expressed herself pleased, and endorsed
all that had been said. Conference adjourned
for 3 months.
				MARY BRONSON, Sec'y.
				AVIS M. BRONSON Ass't.

	AMELIA WELLS, the Queen of the Gypsies,
is said to be worth $60. 00.

	R. S. Stake Secretaries should remember to
send full reports by the middle of September.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

DRAWING ROOM MEETINGS.

BROMPTON SQUARE.

	On Thursday the 12th of July, a drawing-room
meeting was held at the residence of Miss Cour-
tenay, Brompton Square. Amongst those pre-
sent were Lord Stratheden and Campbell, Col-
onel Morrison, Mr. Newton Scott, Miss Frances
Power Cobbe, Miss Becker, Miss Tod, Miss
Biggs, Miss Courtenay, and several others.
	Miss BECKER, in opening the proceedings, ex-
plained that the object of the meeting was the
extension of the parliamentary franchise to
women who posessed the same qualifications
that entitled men to vote. They desired the
franchise not only as an act of justice, but be-
cause the denial of it created a great deal of
mischief to women and to society. Men often
call upon women to take part of certain duties,
but the part which they ask them to take con-
sisted principally in the relief of suffering, or
the mending or repairing of some blunder. She
hoped women would be willing always to come
forward to relieve suffering; but it was beginning
at least to be seen by the more thoughtful that
the prevention of suffering was more important
than the mere relief of it. To her mind women
should have a voice in the prevention of suffer-
ing as well as take part in the alleviation of it
when it existed. The Woman's Suffrage So-
ciety was very earnest in pressing forward the
movement of giving the suffrage to women, and
in endeavoring to circulate a knowledge of its
principles; and it was by means of gatherings
like the present meeting that those principles
were brought forward into quarters which the
ordinary channels—public meetings—for dis-
seminating information in regard to the views
of the society did not reach. Much benefit to
the movement might be brought about by such
gatherings as the one they were that day at-
tending. (Hear, hear.)
MISS FRANCES POWER COBBE spoke in sup-
port of the principle. She maintained that the
movement for extending the suffrage to women
had been generally represented by its opponents
as being highly revolutionary. Its opponents
seem to think that if the right of voting were
granted to women, disastrous results would
follow; and the friends of the movement very
naturally, when they heard such things, were
anxious to try and make out that the giving
the suffrage to women would be a very small
affair, and they went back to the ancient per-
iods, when women used to vote, to show that
formerly women did exercise the franchise.
She did not think either of the views represented
by these two classes were exactly the correct
ones. It simply amounted to this, that in all
ages and times the weaker races and classes
were subject to the stronger ones. It was so in
the days of ancient Greece and Rome. In
Rome the poor slaves had to do the work, and
when they attempted to revolt, 6,000 of them
were crucified. When they came to the feudal
times it was the same thing, the serfs being the
subordinate class. The idea which she wanted
to put forward was that the giving of political
rights to the weaker classes was a notion of
modern times. Women in former times evi-
dently had no political rights, but now a period
had arrived in which all classes were beginning
to assert their rights, and therefore, she looked
upon the affording the suffrage to women not
as a wonderful revolution, but simply as an
inevitable step in the gradual progress of free-
dom throughout the world. (Applause.)
Freedom was proceeding from class to class
and from sex to sex, and the consummation
would be reached when men and women en-
joyed equal privileges. It must not be im-
agined that it was a humiliating assertion to
say that women were a weaker class. As
regards enforcing their rights they were cer-
tainly weaker—they could not pull down park

[Column 2]

railings—(a laugh)—and make the same dis-
turbances that men could—and persons were
apt to confound the want of physical power to
asert rights with the lack of power to see
wisely and judiciously what was for the public
benefit. Now in her opinion these were two
distinct things. Miss Cobbe here read some
extracts from Mr. Kinglake's work upon the
Crimean war, pointing out how badly the sick
were managed in the hospitals out at the
Crimea, her object being to show the different
way in which the female brain worked to that
of man in time of difficulty. Everything she
said, was done by the officers to improve matters,
which instead of getting better got worse.
Ultimately female aid was sent for and ob-
tained, and the state of affairs began to bright-
en up. The speaker having read some other
passages from Mr. Kinglake's work eulogising
Miss Nightingale, and referring to the great
faculties possessed by that lady, concluded by
saying that if there were only one or two Miss
Nightingales, there might still however be a 
considerable number of women quite as good as
some of those who at present got a share in the 
government. (Applause.
	Miss TOD remarked that both of the preced-
ing speakers had alluded in an informal way to
the fact that women had taken a part in public
life, and perhaps a considerable part when it
was remembered  what had been done. This
empire could not have been created had it not
been for the large and important share which
women had taken in the efforts and sacrifices
which had been made to build up its grand
history. What they were then asking for wo-
men was much more new in form than sub-
stance. Until a comparatively recent date no
very large number of men of the ordinary
middle and lower classes had a voice in the
affairs of the State, except in an indirect way.
The Reform Bills actually lessened the political
influence of women, unless they too, upon the
same conditions as the men possessed the vote,
were granted the suffrage, because those Bills
gave the men power out of all proportion to
that possessed by women. Till a comparatively
recent date the difference in that amount of
political power possessed by men over women
was not nearly so great as it was at present,
and therefore it was of importance that the
balance should be restored by giving to women
the parliamentary franchise. Women had
duties to perform to the country at large, and
the qualities which they could bring into public
life were those which to a great extent were
needed. She remembered some years ago talk-
ing to a member of the French army who was
in favor of the Empire, and was very much
struck by what that gentleman said when he
stated "that Parliaments and Assemblies were
feminine modes of government." Government
by free and open discussion and not by force—
that was by force of mind rather then physical
strength—was undeniably one in which women
could take their full rights, and it must be re-
membered that the Government of England
was a government by discussion. It was in
Parliament that all the thoughts, feelings, and 
desires, all the wants, wrongs, and interests of
the nation were discussed, and the essence and
value of such discussion was not so much the
work done as the discussion itself—the free in-
terchange of opinion amongst reasonable people.
It was the duty of women to claim their rights,
and she wished to impress upon ladies that they
ought not to set aside their duties. It was nec-
essary that they should assert their rights, and
it was the duty of every woman householder to
endeavor to obtain for herself and for her sisters
the parliamentary franchise. (Hear, hear.)
	The discussion was continued by Mr. Newton
Scott, Miss C. A. Biggs, Miss Cobbe, Miss Tod,
Miss Becker, Colonel Morrison, and Miss Cour-
tenay, and after partaking of refreshment the
party separated.


FORE STREET. 
	On July 9th a meeting was held at the house
of Mrs. Vialls, Forestreet. Miss Downing and
others addressed those assembled,and a petition
in favor of the resolution was adopted.—Wom-
en's Suffrage Journal.


56	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


RUSTICATING.

	There lies in the southeastern part of Utah,
high up in the mountains, a pretty lake called
Panguitch, or by some Fish Lake; about two
miles long by a mile wide. Its depths are lit-
erally stocked with fresh pinky-colored sal-
mon trout. We are up here paying a sum-
mer's visit, and right pleasant it is, too. I
have traveled through the territory a great deal,
but the scenery near this lake surpasses all
that I have ever seen in Utah in grandeur and
beauty. One scene is gemmed in my memory.
A little vale, soft carpeted with changing
greens, surrounded by the mountain tops,
whose sides hold sentinel-pines that rise in
lofty pride and frown upon the quaken-asp
that trembles at their wrath. The little vale is
checkered here and there with silvery spots
that show which way the streamlet runs.
Across the shepherd drives his murmuring
sheep into the fold, and the lone hut bids wel-
come home with open door and curling smoke
to weary travelers. The pale blue sky, with
banks of dusky clouds, the lovely scene spread
out below, the hush and calm of Nature's bo-
som thrills me with unknown delight.	*	*
I sit here in the doorway of our unpretentious
cottage, and at my feet the lake is rippling
and curling as only lakes can do. Sometimes
I see reflected in its calm clear eyes the brown-
faced mountains opposite. Around the lake
rise wondrous hills with wooded tops. A little
valley with its light green meadows goes up to
meet the mountains on the west. Around,
about, and overhead the scene is beautiful.
Sometimes at twilight, the boat comes in to
shore, and o'er the waters floats the sound of
music. My eyes are filled with tears, and as
the singers chant, "Nearer, my God, to Thee,"
my heart is all too full for utterance. Some-
times we ride up in the canyons, and one such
trip I may describe to you, although my words
are tame and poor, compared with what the eye
would tell to you were you but here. Get in
our wagon and we will take you to the pretty
spring, whose depth gives it the name of "Blue
Spring." The road winds through the pines,
past running streams, and crosses over a mea-
dow to the hills beyond. We climb up a half
mile, swing round a little plateau, and here we
are. A perfect circle full 50 feet across holds
down in its bosom a faultless spring of ice cold
water. We scramble down the rim and walk
out on the pine tree flung from side to side, and
look down into this lakelet's pretty eyes. We
gather at its brink wild roses and geraniums,
and climbing up its sides we spread our shawls
beneath the trees, and drink in all the loveli-
ness of this lone spot. The wind tells mourn-
ful stories in the pines. One tale it tells is this:
A maiden in the far-off olden time, with sweet-
est eyes of blue, laid all she loved beneath the
sod. Down her cheeks there flowed a stream
of tears, and raising oft her eyes, she prayed to
Jove to let her watch over that dear grave for-
ever. The prayer was heard, and eyes of blue
were circled round with earth's broad arm and
down the hill, the tears flowed as a silver
stream, while lofty trees caught up the maid-
en's voice, and with their boughs bore to each
other the oft-told tale of love and constancy,
and so the eyes of blue, the sighing voice are
ever near the spot.			HOMESPUN.
