SALT LAKE CITY  FEBRUARY 15, 1882


IN MEMORIAM
	ELIZABETH ANN WHITNEY.
 She has gone to the realms of an infinite rest,
		We shall hear her sweet voice no more;
	She has joined the bright ranks of the happyand blest,
		With the Just who hare died before.
	And she’ll sing in the mansions of glory above,
		Of Jesus, His power to save;
	His wonderful mission of peace and of love,
		And His victory over the grave.

	Mother Whitney has gone to rest. Yea, she
has gone to that rest that remains to a Saint of
God. She has passed the ordeal that ushers the
(pure spirit into another and higher state of
being. The tenderly beloved, the angelic peace-
maker, the sweet songstress of Zion, has left us
 to join the innumerable, invisible company who
wait just the other side the vail to see God's pur-
poses fulfilled, that they may return again to
enjoy a full fruition of glory celestial.
	Elizabeth Ann Whitney, well known to the
Church for her faithfulness, integrity and good
works, departed this life to-day, Feb. 15, 1882, at
ten minutes past two p. m., at the residence of
her son-in-law, Isaac Groo, in this city, after an
illness of four weeks, during which period she
sometimes suffered extreme pain for days to-
gether. Always patient, sweet and gentle, sub-
missive to the will of God, often repeating the
words, “Father’s time is the best time.”
	Deceased was the widow of Presiding Bishop
Newel K. Whitney, who died Sept. 23, 1850.
Sister Whitney has been the mother of eleven..
children, six of whom are now living to mourn
the loss of an exemplary parent, whose life of
purity and excellence will be for generations to
come an example worthy of emulation through-
out all Israel. A heavenly influence ever seemed
to surround her like a halo, and saint or sinner
could not approach her without feeling in some
degree its power.
	Sister Whitney was one of the tried and
proven friends of the Prophet Joseph Smith,
and she loved to talk of him and the principles
he had taught and the associations connected
with his memory.
	In our next Issue we will give a sketch of her
life.


﻿HOSTILE MEASURES. 

	The bills now pending in the Congress of the
United Stales, asking for adverse legislation and
proposing hostile measures for Utah, are of such
a nature as to strike with amazement those who
know the purity of life and principle that charac-
terize the true Latter-day Saint. Not only so, but
every person possessing the true spirit of patriot-
ism must see how directly opposed these hostile
measures are to the Constitution of the United
States. Are there none in the halls of Congress,-
no men who dare, speak out in defense of the
oppressed, their fellow-creatures and fellow-


countrymen? Is it possible that men and women
are to be condemned to suffer as criminals with-
out trial, or the opportunity of defending them-
selves against assertions made and confirmed by
their enemies? Is not this a direct contradiction
of the laws laid down to govern this free Re-
public; where even criminals guilty of the high-
est offence must be judged before they can be
condemned? Are the women of Utah not to be
allowed to say a word in their own defense, while
those opposed to the principles they have es-
poused can be heard through every newspaper
in the land, and their cause proclaimed from
every pulpit?
	Would it not be well for the nation to pause,
ere any irrevocable step is taken, that would
strike at the peace and happiness and individual
liberty of the people of Utah, and examine well
and closely into the condition of the Territory?
Would it not be wiser to hear both sides of this
case, before decisive steps are taken to punish?
We have always understood every person in the 
Republic was entitled to a fair hearing, before
any verdict could be given.
	At the present time the Territory of Utah is
in a prosperous condition, full of peaceful, happy
homes. Shall these homes be made desolate,
these wives widows, by a fate more cruel than
death, innocent children be denied the father's
love and protecting care, and the tender ties of
family affection be dissolved and destroyed, to
gratify the demands of office seekers and reli-
gious perscutors? Let not the wails of women
and children be heard where now joy sits smil-
ing at the threshold, and the angel of peace at
the hearthstone. What balm has Congress to
offer for  broken hearts and desolated homes?
	This people so greatly misunderstood and
basely misrepresented, have already suffered
cruel wrongs and injustice in years that are past,
have been driven from State to State, despoiled
of their goods, robbed of their homes and inher-
itances, and thousands have died in consequence
of the hardships and sufferings inflicted upon
them because of their religious belief and insti-
tutions. Will not the past suffice? Is this spirit
of religious intolerance in accordance with the
sayings of the Savior, who declared that not a
sparrow could fall to the ground without His
Father's notice?
	This people at the present time are threatened
on every hand. Measures have been suggested
to take away from them every right and liberty
vouchsafed by the Constitution, and these mea-
sures enforced would scarce leave them the right
of conscience. But remember, great and mighty
law-makers and rulers of the nation, that "What
measure ye mete it shall be measured to you
again, pressed down and running over."
	It has been said, and we had firmly believed
that America was the home of the oppressed of
all nations, the land of religious liberty. How
is it to day? Oh, "how have the mighty fallen!"


EDITORIAL NOTES.
				     -----
	We respectfully ask our subscribers, to excuse
the delay of the Woman’s Exponent this issue,
as it was in consequence of the illness and death
of dear Mother Whitney.
	The Ladies’ Lecture Committee appointed at
the Young People’s Conference, Jan. 22, are
prepared to attend ta any calls made for lectures
in the different Associations.
	The Legislators are diligently busy at work,
notwithstanding Congress talks of putting this
Territory into the hands of five men, who will
probably undo all their proceedings.
	The address of the Secretary of the General
President of Relief Society is, Mrs. Sarah M.


Kimball, P. O. Box 785, Salt Lake City, Utah.
General President Y. L. M. I. A., Mrs. Elmina
S. Taylor, P. O. Box 625, Salt Lake City, Utah.

	On Monday, Feb. 6, a Primary Association
was organized in the Fifth Ward of this city,
with Mrs. Myra Winters, President. Sister
Eliza R. S. Smith officiated in the organization,
giving much good instruction, and afterwards
Sister M. I. Horne addressed the parents and
children upon the benefits resulting from these
institutions.


﻿ON Wednesday evening, Feb. 8, a Social Pic-
nic Party convened in the Lion House, this city,
under the auspices of the 18th Ward Relief So-
ciety, Ellen Barton, President, President John
Taylor and ladies, also Apostle Erastus Snow,
Presidents E. R. Snow Smith and M. I. Horne,
invited guests, were present. At about 7 o’clock
p. m., from one hundred and fifty to two hun-
dred people were seated around tables laden with
the bounties of the earth; after which the eve-
ning was spent in a truly social manner, in con-
versation, interspersed with instrumental solos,
songs, speeches and recitations. The spirit of
love and union prevailed, in which all partici-
pated, until 11 o’clock p. m., when the assem-
blage was dismissed with prayer by Elder John
Nicholson
	HANNAH M. WRIGHT, Sec'y pro tem.
		______________________


OBITUARIES.
				-----
	We regret to chronicle the death of our es-
teemed and beloved sister, Eliza A. Thomas,
who departed this life Jan. 14, 1882, after an ill-
ness of one week with scarlet fever.  Deceased
was born in Heber City, Utah, Nov. 9, 1863.
She was baptized when eight years old, re-
baptized October 2, 1881. Was a member of the
Sunday School and Y. L. M. I. A., and well
known for her sterling qualities, and the news
of her death cast a feeling of sadness over the
entire community.
	The funeral services were held in the 4th
Ward, Provo.  Karl G. Maeser and V. L.
Halliday were the speakers on the solemn occa-
sion; members of the Young Men’s Association
acted as pall bearers.  We sincerely condole
with the relatives and friends of our departed
sister.

		RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whsreas, God in His providence has called
from our midst our much esteemed and respected
sister and co-laborer in the cause of improve-
ment; and,
	Whereas, Realizing the great loss sustained
by the Association and the promoters of the
cause of truth, and especially him to whom she
plighted her troth, her parents, relatives and
friends; therefore,
	Be it resolved, That we do sincerely condole
with them in this their great afflction and be-
reavement, and tender to them our heartfelt
sympathy.
	Resolved, That we unanimously adopt these
resolutions as a token of respect and high appre-
ciation of her sterling qualities.
	Resolved, That these resolutions be spread
upon the minutes of the Association, and that
a copy be forwarded to her parents, and that
they be published in the Woman’s Exponent.

					Amos D. Holdaway,
					George Elliot.
	Committee,		Herbert S. Pyne,
					Emma Twelves,
					Hannah Arrowsmith,
					Maggie Watsson,


﻿Died, in Provo City, Jan. 27, 1882, Susan Adah
Howe, daughter of James J. and Martha Price
Howe.  She was born in Surrey Co., North
Carolina, Sept. 6, 1886. Deceased was a resident
of Timpanogus, but was attending school in
Provo, at the B. Y. Academy. She was a young
lady highly respected and esteemed by all who
knew her; a loving and faithful friend, a kind
sister and a dutiful child.
					EMMA BROWN.

	Died at Mt. Pleasant, Sanpete Co., Feb. 4,
1882, Sister Sarah K. Wells. Deceased was born
in Denmark, Nov. 3, 1793, and emigrated to this
country in 1863. Sister Sarah has been a good
and faithful woman, and true to every principle
of the Gospel. She was a member of our Relief
Society, and took great pleasure in doing good
wherever an opportunity presented itself.  But
she has gone to rest in her eighty-ninth year,
like a shock of corn fully ripe, to await a glorious
resurrection.				M. F. C. Morrison
				-----
	Died, in Monroe, Sevier Co. on the 9th day of
Jan., 1832, Sister Geneva Collings, beloved wife
of Wm. Collings, and daughter of Geo. W. and
Mary Bean.
	Sister Genova was in the bloom of woman-
hood, aged 22 years. Was married at the last
April Conference.  She died in childbed, leaving
a little daughter five days old. She died, as she
had lived, a noble woman and a faithful Saint.
Her kindness of manner, generosity of heart
and integrity to her religion won all hearts.
She had only to be known to be loved. She
leaves a host of friends, who sincerely mourn
her loss and condole with her bereaved husband
and parents.			CELIA E. BEAN.
	Richfield, Jan. 29, 1882.

INSTRUCTIONS
TO THE, SECRETARIES OF THE RELIEF SOCIETY;
	IN REPLY TO FREQUENT INQUIRIES.
	It is very desirable that a uniformity should
exist throughout all the branches of the Relief
Society in regard to the Secretaries’ Books.
	In some business departments of the Relief
Society, we are under the necessity of varying,
in order to meet the varied local circumstances
of the different branches; but this does not, in
any instance, apply to the Secretaries.
	In each department we want to learn to do
business in the best possible manner, and to
adopt the manner in which it can be done with
the greatest facility and the least labor.
	The Secretaries of the various Branches of the
Belief Society are, or should be, historians of
their respective Branches. One Book (or when
one Book is full another succeeds it) should con-
tain the whole history—all records of the Branch;
and those records should comprise everything
worthy of preservation, and in as concise a man-
ner as practicable. In this, skill and Judgment
are requisite, and “practice makes perfect.”
	In the first place - at the meeting the Secre-
tary should take minutes of all business transac-
tions and whatever is said or done that should
be read at the next meeting; all that has been
donated to the society since the date of the last
meeting, with the name in full of each doner;
the amount of each cash donation, and the price
of each article donated. These figures properly
placed in the margin, are readily footed in mak-
ing up reports: and all proceedings are readily
referred to under their respective dates.
	Should the Branch have an organized Board
of Teachers (which is unnecessary in small
Branches), and that Board a Secretary, a copy
of the minutes of each teachers' meeting must


be forwarded to the general Secretary in time
for her to compile under date whatever is worthy
of record, in its proper order in Book.
	In making up Quarterly or Annual Reports,
the Treasurer will furnish the Secretary with a
list of all Disbursements from date of last report,
which, after having been read and accepted by
the Branch, also read at the Conference for
which it was prepared, the Secretary will copy
it under date, and thus have in one Book the
minutes of each meeting, all the receipts and
disbursements, comprising all business matters
of the Branch.
	The foregoing is in accordance with the origi-
nal sample Book, which was fully approved by
the Prophet Joseph Smith, through whom the
revelation was given for the organization of the
Relief Society.
					E. R. SNOW SMITH.
	S. L. City,  January, 1882.


[Column 1]
		THE PRAYER OF FAITH.
	We regret to learn that Brother Tibbet's
earthly career has ended; but while we sympa-
thize with his sorrowing family, a ray of joy bursts
forth, in remembrance that he died in full faith
in the Gospel. And not only so, but having a
knowledge that his partner, Sister Tibbets, has
a strong testimony of its truth. This I learned
from herself. She told me that while in Eng-
land a tumor, or cancer, began to make its ap-
pearance, and was so painful that she had no
rest. She went to consult a physician, who told
her he would undertake the cure, but it was a
painful operation, and he could not promise the
result. This alarmed her, and she resolved to
call upon the Elders to administer to her. They
came, and at teh same time the doctor came
also; she introduced him to the Elders, and told
him why they had come. "Well," said he, "if
they cure you I can send them several others,
for they are very difficult to cure."The physi-
cian left, and the Elders administered according
to the pattern our Savior has given, using oil in
the name of Jesus. The result was a perfect
cure. She told me herself that she never felt a
pain or a twinge from that day to this.
	This reminds me of the first time I saw the
oil administered in the name of the Lord. It
was at the house of Sister Catharine Clawson,
in Utica, New York. She is the mother of Bro.
H. B. Clawson, who was living in the city of
Utica at the time. We had been baptized one
week previously under the hands of Elder James
Blakesley, who brought the Gospel to Utica in
the year 1842. Sister Clawson, with three of
her sisters and two nieces besides myself, were
baptized Sept. 15, 1842. The next Sabbath, just
after morning service, Sister Ruth Reese (after-
wards wife of Heber C. Kimball) remarked that
she was going to see Catharine, because her
child was sick, which would prevent her from
attending meeting that morning. I asked to go
with her, another asked the same privilege, when
Elder Blakesley inquired, "May I go?" "Cer-
tainly," was the reply. After arriving and be-
ing seated, Elder B. says: "If your clock was
out of repair, who do you think would be the
best repairer?" We looked at each other, and
wondered why he asked this question. He no-
ticed our embarassment, and said: "Would not
the maker of the clock be the best repairer?"
"Certainly," was the reply. "Then the Maker
of this little frame is the best repairer." The
mother then said, "Will you administer to the
child?" He replied, "Let us pray." We all
knelt, when he offered up a prayer; then arising
he laid his hands upon the head of the child,
and prayed God to restore it to health. She did
not open her eyes, or notice anything, until he

[Column 2]
had finished his prayer, when she immediately
looked up at him, then reached her hands to her
mother, who took her up. She asked for a
drink of water, after which she called for some-
thing to eat, and then got down to play, looking
so cheerful and happy. This was Bro. H. B.
Clawson's youngest sister, Harriet, now living.
	Sister Ruth Kimball will remember this cir-
cumstance as well as myself.  I must say that I
was delighted with the thought that the Lord
could hear and answer prayer as anciently.  I
well remember my impression, which I gave
utterance to; it was this: To be healed like that
when I was sick would be the greatest testimony
I could ask; to know that I was accepted of the
Lord, and had been so greatly blessed by Him.
	It was two years from that time when I was
taken violently sick of a fever. My parents
lived on a farm five miles from Nauvoo at this
time. I had been sick four days and nights,
and my mother was doing her best to break the
fever. Finally some one asked if I had been ad-
ministered to. No, I had not thought of this,
but said I would. The next impression was,
you will not be healed; you are not as innocent
as that little child, and you have not lived faith-
ful. No, I know it, thought I, but I will be
blessed, for a blessing attends every effort we
make in keeping the commands of our Father.
Accordingly, two Elders came from the harvest
field; one was Emer Harris, a brother of Martin
Harris, the other my step-father, Bro. Dunlap.
But I found I could not sit up; mother sat and
held me up in bed. They laid their hands upon
my head. I wept, for I felt unworthy of the bless-
ing I sought. But in the midst of the prayer
my sorrow was turned to joy. I was healed. I
felt strong, I could sit alone and fan myself,
could eat with a good relish, and slept sweetly
all night, without awaking once. I was so
happy; I thought it the brightest day I had ever
seen in my life.
	Such is the effect of the prayer of faith since
the Gospel has been restored to the earth. And
such are the results when we come to our Heav-
enly Father in a humble, childlike manner, ask-
ing as a hungry child would do for a piece of
bread to satisfy the demands of nature. Yet
we are told there are many gifts given to each
one severally, as He will. And why? Because
we are all members of one body, and each one
must fill his or her sphere of action, and be per-
fect, even as our Father in Heaven is perfect.
How is that to be done? I understand it is to
fill our sphere as perfectly as God our Father
fills his, which is the earnest prayer of
					M. E. KIMBALL.


BIRTHDAY PARTY

  In Ogden City, Feb. 7, 1882, it being the
sixty-eighth anniversary of Mrs.  Charlotte
Etherington's birthday, her children and some
friends met at her residence in the 2d Ward,
taking plenty of the good things of the earth
with them, and making her some presents,
which were duly appreciated.  After the boun-
teous repast was over, speeches were made ap-
propriate to the occasion.
   When Mrs. Etherington arrived in this val-
ley twenty-five years ago she had five children;
since that time she has become grandmother to
forty-three grandchildren and two great-grand-
children,  twenty-two boys and twenty-three
girls; the total number of children, grandchil-
dren, etc., is something near sixty souls. The
evening was a very enjoyable one, and long to
be remembered. Among other entertainments
a poem was read, which had been composed
for the occasion by Mrs. Mary Ann Pingree,
expressing the love and good feelings of the
sisters and friends.					P,


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
			REPORTS

		SANPETE STAKE

  The Quarterly Conference of R. S. of San-
pete Stake was held at Spring City, Dec. 15,
1881, Prest. M. A. P. Hyde presiding. Prest.
Peterson and one of his Counselors, Henry Beal,
also other brethren from adjacent settlements,
were present. After devotional services the
reports were read, showing that a creditable
amount had been appropriated for the erecting 
of a woolen factory, also for the Manti Temple
and other purposes. A change was made in
Stake President of Primary Association, Sister
Helena Madsen taking the place of Sister
Phebe Maiben, who resigned. A committee
was set apart to take charge of means already
received and to be raised for the establishment
of the factory. The presidents of different 
wards reported the members of their respec-
tive societies as being alive to all known duties 
as members of the Church and Kingdom of 
God.  Prest. Hyde addressed  the  assembly 
upon the storing of grain and the advancement
of the factory; also urged them to be diligent
in keeping their covenants unbroken.
   President Petersen heartily endorsed all the
proceedings, encouraged the sisters in their
financial and charitable enterprises.  A num-
ber of brethren and sisters made brief remarks,
encouraging and stimulating the sisters to more
faithfully honor their high and holy callings.
Choir sang, Doxology.  Benediction.  Confer-
ence adjourned, to be held at Ephraim, March
15, 1882.
			FANNY KOFFORD, Cor. Sec'y.

			JUAB STAKE
   The Juab Stake organization of R. S. held
their Fifteenth Quarterly Conference Jan. 20,
at the Nephi Meeting House, commencing at 2
P. M., pursuant to adjournment, Mrs. Amelia
H. Goldsbrough presiding. Most of the Stake
and local officers were present, though some
were detained on account of sickness.
   After the usual opening exercises, the min-
utes of the last quarterly conference were read
and approved. Then followed verbal reports
by the presidents of the different branches,
which proved that the sisters were energetic in
the discharge of their duties, and were alive
to the interest of the great Latter-day work.
   Financial reports were read, which showed
the condition of the branches to be very good,
there being no lack of means to supply the
wants of the needy.
   We then had some excellent remarks from
Presidents A. H. Goldsbrough, M. Pitchforth,
S. Aagard, H. Grover, and Counselors J. Pic-
ton, M. Bryan and Sister Hendrickson. All
spoke spiritedly, and touched on many subjects
of interest. Some of them on the training of
children, the responsibility of parents and the
hurtful results arising from neglect of these
duties. Thought if we would be faithful we
had no cause to fear from our enemies. A
very few remarks from Brothers Brown and
Kendall. 
   It was then moved and carried that a report
of the Conference be forwarded to the EXPO-
NENT.  Conference adjourned for three months.
Singing, "Hark the song of jubilee." Bene-
diction by Bishop Aagard.
				A. L. BIGLER, Cor. Sec'y
			PINE VALLEY.
   The sisters of Pine Valley Relief Society,
with three of the brethren R. L. Lloyd, John
Grey and James Bracken, (our Bishop being
absent from home, met Jan. 15, 1882, at 11
o'clock a. m., at the residence of Mrs. Dorinda
M. Slade, President of the Relief Society, to 
celebrate and surprise her on her seventy-fourth
birthday, Brother Bracken being sixty-six the
day previous, the 14th. Tables were set, and
seventeen sisters and the above named brethren,
twenty in all, sat down to a sumptuous repast,
all in excellent spirits. Brother Bracken re-
turned thanks. It was snowing very heavily.
Dinner over, the time was spent in conversing,
and the song, "Hard times come again no
more," was rendered.
   Meeting commenced at two p. m. Singing,
"Come, come, ye Saints."  Prayer by D. M.
Slade. Singing, "Up, awake, ye defenders of 
Zion." President Slade then gave the meeting
into the hands of the company assembled.
Sister Sophia M. Burgess then read a letter
composed by Ellen M. Jones, and inscribed to
Sister Slade, in behalf of the sisters of Pine
Valley Relief Society.
DORINDA M. SLADE, PREST. R. S.
   Dear Sister--We the sisters of Pine Valley
R. S., have met to-day, Jan. 15, to celebrate
the birthday of our beloved and much re-
spected President, Dorinda M. Slade, it being
her 74th birthday. And we feel to ask God,
our Heavenly Father, to bless, comfort and
cheer her lone way, that He may prolong her
days upon the earth, that she may  still be use-
ful in her day and generation, that she may
teach and instruct the sisters and the youth in
those things they may have a desire to learn;
and that He will bless us while we enjoy our-
selves this day.  We will pray God, our Heav-
enly Father, to remember, in an especial man-
ner, "thine Israel" under all circumstances,
throughout the length and breadth of the
whole earth. Bless thy servant John Taylor,
his Counselors and the Quorum of the Twelve,
with wisdom, light and intelligence, that they
may preside and lead "thy people" in the ways
of truth and righteousness.  Remember our
Delegate to Congress, and all that pertaineth
unto him; shield and protect him from the
power of his enemies; let thine influence and
power be round about him, as a wall of fire,
that they may have no power or dominion over
him; may he ride triumphant over them. Re-
member the honest in heart, this day in all the
earth, and all who have a desire to do thy will
and keep thy commandments. We, the sisters
of Pine valley, have a desire to live according
to thy mind and will and to join heart and
hand with Sister Slade in assisting to promote
the interest, happiness and welfare of the poor
and needy of our ward, to comfort and cheer
the tempted and tried, the broken-hearted, and
all that need assistance. We will ever be on
hand as a band of sisters to magnify our call-
ing and perpetuate the R. S. and the work of 
God to the ends of the earth. And may God
bless our President her Counselors and the 
members, and may Sister Slade live to be an
honored instrument in the hands of God in
doing good all the day long, is the wish and
fervent prayer of her sister in the Gospel,
					ELLEN M. JONES.

   Brother Bracken said he well remembered
"the wrongs of Missouri" and "the fate of Nau-
voo," and spoke by the spirit of inspiration; he
was followed by Brothers R. L. Lloyd and
John Grey. Singing, "How have the nations
grown corrupt." Then the following sisters
bore testimony to the truth of this work: Sis-
ters Jones, Ann Snow, Amanda Burgess and
Sister Jacobson. Singing, "O Saints have you
seen." Sisters Sophia Burgess, Jane Gardner
and Cynthia Gardner each spoke a short time.
   We had without exception one of the most
enjoyable times we have ever had the pleasure
of witnessing. The Spirit of God was richly
enjoyed by all present. Singing, "O ye moun-
tains High." Benediction by R. L. Lloyd.
					ELLEN M. JONES.

			BEAR LAKE
   Synopsis of Conference of Relief Societies
of the Bear Lake Stake of Zion, held Sunday,
Jan. 15, 1882.  Present of the stand--Wm.
Budge and James H. Hart, of the Presidency
of teh Stake; Presidents Julia Lindsay and
Elizabeth Collins, of the Stake Relief Socie-
ties; President Nancy Pugmire, of the Stake
Y. L. M. I. A.; President A. Laker, of the
Stake Primary Associations;  Bishop J. A. 
Hunt; Presidents of the branch Relief Socie-
ties, Y. L. M. I. Associations and Primary As-
sociations, and others. Singing, "High on the
Mountain Tops," etc. Prayer by Prest. Wm.
Budge. Singing, "With Joy We Own Thy Ser-
vants, Lord."
   President Lindsay felt thankful for the good
circumstances that attended the opening of the
conference, and called the presidents of the
branch Relief Societies to the stand.  The fol-
lowing societies were then reported: St. Charles,
Mary Jacobs; Bloomington, Catherine Nelson;
Paris, First Ward, M. Innes; Ovid, M. Carrol-
son; Fish Haven, F. Howell; Garden City, A.
E. Cook; Laketown, M. E. Findlay. A writ-
ten report from Liberty was read. Paris, 2d
Ward,  Woodruff, Randolph,  Bennington,
Georgetown and Saleratus Creek Societies were 
not represented.
   President Lindsay explained why the Statis-
tical and Financial Reports were not read this
morning. Spoke on the principle of order,
and requested the sisters to forward all contri-
butions to the Temple to her, with the names
of the donators, so that she might forward the
same to the President of the Stake. We have
to be energetic and persevering in order to ac-
complish what the Lord requires at our hands.
   The Financial and Statistical Reports of the 
different branch societies were then read.
   Singing, "Before Jehovah's Awful Throne."
Conference adjourned until 2 p. m.  Benedic-
tion by President Hart. 
   2 p. m.  Singing, "O Awake my Slumbering
Minstrel." Prayer by Bishop Hunt. Singing,
"Except the lord Conduct the Plan." The
sacrament was then administered, during which
the choir sang, "Ye Children of our God" and
"O God, the Eternal Father".
  The following reports were then read:
   Statistical Report for the half year ending
Dec. 31, 1881: Teachers, 123; members, 458;
total officers and members, 629; meetings held,
77; average attendance, 180; Church papers,
161; WOMAN'S EXPONENT, 35.
   Financial Report: On hand at last report,
cash, $37 35; property, $161 81, wheat, 387bu., 17 lbs. Receipts: Cash, $248 53; property,
$19 10; wheat, 34 bus., 10 lbs. On hand:
Cash, $88 55; property, $758 25; wheat, 707
bus., 37 lbs.
   President P. Hoganson, of Montpelier, and
President Sparks, of Dingle Dell, made brief
remarks.
   President Lindsay said there are a great
many duties devolving upon the sisters of the
Relief Societies.  Related her early experience
in connection with the Relief Societies in Kays
Ward. While in one of those meetings the
spirit of prophecy rested upon her, but through
timidity she neglected this spirit, and it left 
her. She instantly became darkened in mind, 
while another sister in the meeting arose and
gave the same prophecy that the Spirit of God
dictated to the speaker. She then made a
covenant that she would never again neglect
the whisperings of the Spirit of God. No
person can conscientiously sit down in Zion
contented and say there is nothing to do.
There is an abundance of work to accomplish,
and our time should be spent in helping to do
it. Spoke of the negligent condition of some
of the sisters in not attending their Relief So-
ciety. Every one that neglects the meetings,
loses a certain portion of the Spirit of God.
The Lord has placed a great duty, as mothers,


upon-us-in-training up.our.children.in the fear
and admonition of the Lord, and if we neglect
this duty,“the Lord will surely-hold-us account-
able.,.. Urged upon the, mothers to teach their
children-, the: importance of seeking unto the
Lord for a testimony .concerning'the truth of 
this work and :the.-prophetic mission of Joseph
Smith. We must he charitable with each
other, and bear with one another’s weaknesses.
Gaveimuch valuable.instruction in relation to,
the principle .of polygamy, and advised all
never. to fight against this, sacred ordinance.
Sisters,xnever influence your daughters against
this. Ifi we-live--our religion properly, there
will.never’be trouble in our ‘ families.
AVeshould never interfere with one another’s
family relations, because it invariably brings
strife -and -discord; Rather be peacemakers,-
for “blessed are the peacemakers for they shall
see God.” Cultivate our minds, and we will
find it to our advantage, both in this world and
in that to come; Imparted much valuable in-
struction-to the. teachers of Relief Societies,
and’acquainted them jvith their duties. Slidwed
how the sisters might gain the blessings of God,
and closed by invoking the blessings of God
upon all present. '
President Collins felt thankful to God that
He had given her strength to.be in the midst of
the, people. If we do not do our duties to
God and men, we cannot expect to gain those
blessings that they enjoy who live with an eye
single before God.
	President Wm. Budge said he had listened
to the,reports that, had' been read- with great

F&ther; "Hoped the mothers .Would calL their

WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE CONVENETION.

		STATE OF NEW YORK.

			[Column 1]
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
	I had the great pleasure, in company with
Sisters Zina D. H. Young and Dr. Ellen B.
Ferguson, of attending the Woman's Suffrage
Convention for the State of New York, held
Feb. 2 and 3, in Chickering Hall, corner of
18th St. and Fifth Avenue. It is a magnifi-
cent Hall, worthy of the cause promulgated
within its walls, and the paying of one hundred
dollars a day for its occupation for two days,
shows the high estimation of the work in hand
and also the praiseworthy executive ability of
the gracefull queen of wit and fable, Mrs. Lillie
Devereux Blake, the present President of the
New York Association.
	I did not attend the reception given in the
Stuyvesant Hotel, which I am informed was a
brilliant affair.
	The morning of the second day (I did not
attend the first) was occupied principally in
hearing reports from the different counties,
mostly represented by Mrs. Blake herself.
	The afternoon was occupied by Mr. Wilcox,
Mrs. Slocum, Mrs. Joslyn Gage and a witty
German lady from Albany, and representative
of that branch of the work. Her speech was
full of good, common-sense hits, and reminded
me much of our dear, sweet Camilla Cobb at
home. Mr. Wilcox is truly woman's champion,
and the woman must be truly proud and happy
(the sole interpretation of the word Marriage)
who calls him husband; and  the cause will, in
the hour of its success, owe much to his untir-
ing efforts. May he live to realize his well-
grounded, sanguine hopes, and wear the laurels
which will be undisputably his due.
	Mrs. Slocum's interesting speech was imbued
throughout with the earnest pathos and elo-
quence born of full conviction of the truthful-
ness and deep-rooted blessings to humanity,
that would spring from the accomplishment
of the enfrachnisement of women. Her placid
and peaceful face shone with her good will to
all women; and her beautiful, silvery hair was
tenderly suggestive of the long time she had
labored for the good of her sisters. We have
reason to love those veterans, one and all. She
touched as did every woman speaker, upon the
soul-thrilling mother question of right to the
possession of children. Shades of Nero! If
there is one thing in which a woman (a good
one of course) feels secure in, and of which
she thinks no one has a shadow of a right to
dispute her supremacy, it is her possession of
the children of her bosom. Our sacred Guide
Book tells us we are rewarded according to our
works, and what other reward could even be
suggested to a mother's heart than that of pos-
session of her children? If only the one ar-
gument of capacity was considered, magnanim-
ity could render but one verdict, Give them to
her. Were all men model husbands (and the
sad truth is that scarcely the majority are)
these vexed questions would never have arisen.
Not that I wish to be understood that women
are never in fault, I am far from being so ultra
as that, but we know that evils are and were
meant to be that we may be able to appreciate
heaven by comparison; and the sole struggle is
how to so adjust rights and privileges that
every one may enjoy some approach at least to
justice.
	Mrs. Josyln Gage, with her snowy crown of
womean's glory, must be heard to be appre-
ciated. Her wealth of scholarly lore is a bea-
con light to all women of her age, and to those
coming after her, encouraging them to press
onward in cultivating the germs of intelligence
given of God liberally to daughters as well as
sons. If I did not misunderstand her (and I
hope I did), she over-reached herself just once,

			[Column 2]
by making the assertion that if all things were
fair play, woman was man's superior. I regard
this as one step beyond the one desirable end
 of equality. For woman to assert herself as
the superior would be reversing the strict or-
ganization of God Himself upon the earth, as
taught us in Holy Writ, and also evidenced by
facts as they stand in Nature, unalterable either
by the feeble voice of man or woman. Man,
by his superior physique and mental force, was
from the beginning placed at the head, not-
withstanding the man with the largest brain
on record (70 ounces) was an inmate of an in-
sane asylum. This only proves that too much
of a good thing is good for nothing, and that
too much brain, like too much knowledge, may
make a man mad, and perhaps if every woman
had as much brain as the best man, they might
all go mad; at least, I fear some men would
with jealousy; for as a general thing they are
exeedingly "tender" of their supremacy. The
law of equal right now demanded by women
cannot cause any mental pertubation or disturb-
ance in the noble and true man, for he is in-
variably liberal and even solicits that women
be given every opportunity of improving and
making use of every talent her Father has
giver her, and in the almost unconscious en-
joyment of superiority, he is not forever green
with jealousy, as the man is who is writhing
under the humiliating consciousness of his own
inferiority. It is no part of the ambition of
any true  born, to lauch ahead, to launch ahead, and,
looking back over her shoulder, say, "Come on,
husband, and step lively, lest I lose you." So
that equal rights are only as a compensating
checkmate for bad men, and are in no wise to
infringe upon the good.
	After the afternoon session I had the pleasure
of an introduction to Miss Anothony, and a
second one to the noble Mrs. Gage, having
been presented to her previously at the recep-
tion held in the Woman's Pavilion during the
Centennial Exposition, Phila. I regard them
both as women of pleasing and impressive
presence.
			To be continued.


SURPRISE PARTY.
	On Tuesday, the 7th inst, the good sisters of 
the Plain City Relief Society, accompanied by 
the Brass Band, repaired to the residence of
Sister Elmira Raymond, and gave her a  com-
plete surprise.  Sister Raymond has faithfully
performed the duties of President of the R. S.
since its frist organization in this place. After
partaking of picnic, which the sisters took
along, they spent an agreeable time, the band
furnishing its quoto with excellent music.  Be-
fore partaking of picnic, Sister Raymond was
presented with a beautiful album and a pair of
vases;	after which same very pretty verses
were read by Sister Polly Ann Richardson.
	Our Relief Society is in good condition; the
wants of the poor are well supplied.
			SUSANNAH ROBINSON.

The Woman's Silk Culture Association has

 MRS. Ednah D. Cheney has written a chap-
ter on "The Women of Boston," in Mr. Win-
sor's Memorial History of Boston.

	Mrs. Mary E. Haggart, of Indianapolis, on 
Tuesday, the 25th of January, addressed the 
Kentucky Legislature on the rights of women.
The same day a bill was introduced to give
property rights, and rights to children and to
women in a degree never known in Kentucky
before.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE OPPOSING PARTY.
			------
	What meaneth the sound of tumult--this
array of words, trying by the force of their
power to bring trouble upon us? The spirit of
the evil one inspires the wicked, and they are
tools in his hands in this, his last struggle for
power and supremacy. His days are numbered,
his time  short, and with increased force and
venom he raises his hand against the people of
the Lord, the Latter-day Saints. He stirs up
the hearts of his followers to warfare with the
living God. It is not polygamy they seek to
exterminate, but by that as a handle seek to
exterminate the truth and drive it from the
earth, as once before it was driven. Will they
succeed? No, never! they cannot do it, for the
Lord has promsied, and it will endure.
	Howl on, corrupt politicians! Howl on, ye
who ought to be ministers of peace, but who are
ministers of war! The honest in heart will 
stand back aghast at your proceedings, and in
their hearts will condemn your works. Evil
spirits may rage, and Satan may plan, all all
will be confounded, for the Lord has spoken
and His word is true.
	Celestial marraige, that gives us power over
death, that unites in heaven as well as on earth,
that has no dissolution in it, is the present tar-
get. Celestial marraige, that brings the bless-
ings of the Lord, that is a commandment form
heaven, is what they pretend to aim at. Really
it is the kingdom of God, the truth from heaven,
and the people who obey the commandments of
God, against whom their hatred exists. The
same God who heard the prayer of Hezekiah
and smote the army of Senacharib will hear
our prayers and smite those who oppose us.
The same God that delivered Daniel from the
lion's den will deliver us. In Him we trust.
The power of the wicked will be broken, and
righteousness at last prevail.
				EMILY B. SPENCER.


THE MISSES Kollock are four typical western
girls. The family of W. E. and A. M. Kollock,
of Madison, Wis., consists of seven members,
four of whom are sisters. Of these, Dr. Mary
Kollock Bennett, the eldest, graduated at the
Woman's Medical College of Chicago, and for
many years has been practising successfully in
that city. The next, Dr. Harriet Kollock,
graduated in the medical department at Ann
Arbor, Mich., nine years ago, since which time
she has been eminetly successful in her pro-
fessional works. The third, Rev. Florence
Kollock, graduated at Canton Theological
College some years since, and is now doing a
good work as a pastor in a beautiful church,
built for her by the parish during the last two
years, at Englewood, a fine suburban town of
Chicago. Dr. Jennie C. Kollock, the youngest
sister, graduated in the dental department of
Ann Arbor, Michigan, last March, together
with a class of thirty-six gentlemen, she being
the only lady, and passing the highest examin-
ation of any in the class. She is now establish-
ing herself successfully as a dental practitioner
in Chicago.--Ex.

	THE WOMAN SUFFRAGE HEARING.--
A public Legislative hearing was given by
the Joint Special Committee to the petitioners
for Women Suffrage and others, in Boston,
January 30, at 9.30 a.m., in the Green Room
of the State House. It drew together a large
audience, which crowded the hall to its utmost
capacity. Hon. Wm. I. Bowditch, president
of the Massachusetts Woman Suffrage Asso-
ciation, made the opening address with great
force and eloquence.
	Mrs. Lucy Stone spoke with much feeling
of the long thirty years, during which the
friends of Woman Suffrage had come annually
to this State House to ask for equal rights for
the women of Massachusetts. She appealed to 
the committee to make the case of the women
their own. She said: "The torn battle flags in
the rotunda show the strife men have made for
liberty and equal rights for me. We have
no battle flags to show for our cause, but thirty
years of our lives lie behind us devoted to the
great purpose of securing equal rights for
women. We have seen our youth and middle
life pass from us in  vain asking for rights
which all men have without asking, and now,
with old age coming on, we are still petitioning
for this sacred right of suffrage.--Ex.

	Augusta Moore says in the Evangelist: "I
took up a daily paper one day and saw nine-
teen divorces in it. I took up another and saw
five. I went to a certain church and found
there, in full communion, a woan who has 
two husbands. I visited the house of 'a lead-
ing member' in a church, and found there a
minister who has divorced one wife (a virtu-
ous woman), and is married to another. I 
know a minister who married a doctor to a 
woman for whom he had divorced his excellent
wife. This was in Clinton, Ct. Time would
fail me to particularize the cases of this sort
that I know, and I live among the best people
that there are in the world. These cases are
as plenty everywhere as they are about me--
indeed, much more so in many localities. A
friend writes me form California that it is
hardly safe to say 'divorce,' so many of 'the
very nicest people' have two or three husabands
or wives living. Now is not this an encour-
aging state of things? Is it any wonder that
some ministers are telling us how fast the world
is growing better, how the very millennium is
upon us? Is it to be feared we can hardly ex-
ert just the moral influence that is needed over
the Mormons till the States are reformed. Prof.
Austin Phelps says: 'We are not half awake
to the fact that by our laws of divorce and our
toleration of the social evil, we are doing more
to corrupt the nation's heart than Mormonism,
tenfold.'"--Cincinnati Times.

THE SACRAMENT
		BY HANNAH T. KING.
	"The Lord Jesus, the same night in which he was be-
trayed took bread, and when he had given thanks, he
brake it, and said, take, eat, this is my body which is
given for you, this do in remembrance of me. 1 Cor., xi
23 24.
	Betrayed! Oh! who may tell the agony
	That [xxx] upon the meek Redeemer's Soul
	As, in the hush o fhtat last solemn feast
	He looked upon His chosen Ones? Betrayed!--
	He found them wandring in a thirsty land,
	And he had led them unto living streams,
	And he had fed them with the bread of life--
	When crowds hung breathless on His miracles,
	Their post was at His side--in the still hour
	When He was wont to seek the quiet garden--
	Beneath the beauty of the starry sky,--
	Upon the mountain's top, on the wild waves
	That foamed around them in the midnight gloom
	They held communion with Him: Yet He felt
	E'en now, the traitor's kiss upon His cheek!
	The fierce denial sounded in His ear,
	He saw himself forsaken and alone,
	"Thou, even thou, mine own familiar friend"
	Oh! though more piercing than the cruel nail,
	Oh! anguish sharper than the wreathed thorn
	To Him, whose name is Lord whoever felt
	A sorrow like this sorrow?-- Mark ye well
	That pale, male brow so sadly raised to Heaven
	In uncomplaining woe, that tender form,
	Whose every nerve is quivering with intense
	Yet patient agony!--The Savior speaks!
	Peace while we bend in deep and anxious awe
	To catch the lightest murmur of His lips
	In this, His hour of suffering. Lo! He breaks
	And with a blessing gives the bread, as soon
	Aye! very soon His body shall be given
	For all! For us! then holding foth the cup
	Touching memorial of the precious blood
	That, ere another day hath run its course
	Shall stain Gethsemane, and Calvary.
	He says, "Do this in Memory of Me."--
	His gentle, mournful eye is fixed on us
	In pleading earnestness; His outstretch'd hand
	is kindly waiting for us; will we add
	One drop of sorrow to that bitter cup
	So full already? Will we wring the heart
	With yet another pang, that even now
	Is breaking with its load of misery?--
	We call ourselves His followers, we bear--
	His sacred name, shall we betray Him too?--
	No! gracious Master! erring tho' we be
	Unmeet, unworthy, not in vain to us
	Shall be thy las commandment, give that cup,
	That broken bread, we would remember Thee.
Salt Lake City, Xmas., 1881.


[Column 1]
		THE REASON WHY.

	What is the reason that all eyes are turned
toward Utah?  Why is it, while the  whole
civilized world is agitated by questions of
grave, social import, and men who sit in high
places fear and tremble on account of the se-
cret combinations and the dangers that increase
on every hand?  When even the head of the
Chief Magistrate of this enlightened nation
has been laid low by the hand of the assassin,
a would-be maniac?  And alas! it is not the
first, but the second of our presidents that has
basely fallen by assassination.  While crime
stalks abroad, unpunished, through the land,
and increases to such a fearful extent that the
laws of man are inadequate to reach it, and
new and strange diseases are exercising the
minds and battling the skill of the medical
[two unreadable lines printed backwards]

[Column 2]
faculty-for do not men and women constantly
violate the laws of  natural affection, and that
which was once considered the crowning joy of
woman, her motherhood, that honor which the
daughters of God's ancient people desired
above all things is cast aside by many as a 
thing of naught, and almost looked upon as a 
disgrace.  While the disobedience of children
is becoming proverbial, for there are but few,
comparatively, that give to their parents that
respect and reverence that their age and social
relations demand.  When we contemplate these
things, is it any wonder that the whole head is
sick and the whole heart faint?  Can the
Christian world tell us the reason why these
things are so?  Can the politicians solve the
problem?   No, they cannot; for does not bold
infidelity walk with the unblushing front, seeking
to destroy the ancient landmarks, daring to 
trample on the laws of God and bid defiance
to his power, and the thoughts of men wander
hither and thither, and fail to find a resting
place in the systems taught by the theologians
of the day.  Can the Christian world find a
remedy for these evils?  No, they cannot; or
at least they have failed to do so.
	What then is the reason that all eyes are
turned toward Utah?  Towards the handful
who were driven into the wilderness, who under-
took what seemed to be impossibilities to re-
deem this barren region of country from the
wild beast and sage brush, and made the wil-
derness to "blossom as the rose," that they
might serve God according to their consciences,
and enjoy the liberty guaranteed to them by
the Constitution of their country.  For this
privilege they were willing to suffer hunger
and thirst, and many laid their bones by the
wayside; for this they fought the cricket and
the grasshopper, and while themselves suffer-
ing for sufficient food, divided their scanty
portion with the Red Man, that they might
teach him the art of industry and to live in
peace with the white man and each other?
	What, then, have the Mormons done, that
this hue and cry should be raised against them?
Why are their names cast out as evil?  Why
are all kinds of absurd falsehoods manufac-
tured and sent abroad by evil-minded men,
who seek to work us injury, by men who
come into our midst to gain wealth?  They
belong to the same class that cried out in the 
days of Paul.  "Great is Diana of the Ephe-
sians."  Not that they cared for Diana, but
because their craft was in danger, for they got
their wealth by making images for  her shrine.
How can we bear all this misrepresentation?
It is because the Lord has said, "Blessed are
ye when all men speak evil of you falsely for
my name's sake," and notwithstanding all the
trials the Latter-day Saints have gone through
there is one woe they will most certainly escape:
"Wo be unto you when all men speak well of
you."  But why all this intolerance against
the Mormons?  It is because the Lord has
again spoken from the heavens, and set His
hand to bring about His purposes in the last
days.  It is because the little stone that was
cut out of the mountain without hands has
commenced to roll, and will continue to grow
and increase till it shall fill the whole earth,
and none can stop its progress... Man may
rage and imagine all kinds of vain things, but
vainly do men seek to overthrow the designs
of the Almighty, or stay the hand of Him
[two unreadable lines printed backwards]

[Column 3]
who saith to the raging sea, "Be still, thus far
shalt thou go and no farther; here shall thy
proud waves be stayed."
	We put our trust in Him who setteth up
kings and putteth them down, and one shall
dare to say, What doest thou? This is the God
in whom we trust, and He will not suffer His
people to be confounded. This is the 
<i oj="" gnol="" i="" i'k-j.—’i="" "tt'tt-—-—-i="" ,.="">can afford to say to the wicked, and to our
oppressors, Get you gone, I have nothing to do
with you; the reign of righteousness is about
to be ushered in; and I have my work to do,
and hope to play my part in the great consum-
mation.
					PHEBE C. YOUNG.


NOTES AND NEWS.
	A GERMAN lady, Elsie Von Koeber, proposes
an organization of Women for the purpose of
promoting and encouraging female emigration
from Europe.
	MARY L. Haggart asks: "If men are so cor-
rupt that they cannot be permitted to vote with
women, ougt they to be trusted to vote for
and represent women?"
	GOVERNOR Cornell, of New York, says that
"the voting of women at the school elections in
New York has increased the proficiency of the
school and added to their intelligence."
	THE late Mrs. Eliza F. Eddy, of Boston,
Mass., left property valued at about $50,000
to be divided equally between Lucy Stone and
Susan B. Anthony for use in the woman suff-
rage work.
	QUEEN VICTORIA'S speech at the opening
of Parliament  of general interest: "My lords
and gentlemen, it is with much satisfaction that
I again invite your advice and assistance in the
conduct of public affairs. I have given my
approval to a marraige between Price Leop-
old and the Princess Selina of Waldeck. I
have every reason to believe this will be a hap-
py union --Ex

	THE birthday of Susan B. Anthony was cel-
ebrated in Indianapolis Ind.	Feb. 15, at the
residence of Dr. and Mrs. Carey. The papers
speak of it as a charming affair. Many people
of note editors and writers assembled to honor
the labors of this pioneer worker in the cause
of woman suffrage and to give expression to the
feelings of love respect and admiration they
entertain for this able advocate this heroic
champion of equal rights. Miss Anthony has
been before the public thirty-four years, teach-
ing moral responsibilty and citizenship for
women as well as men.
	OSCAR WILDE the apostle of Enlish aes-
theticisin now visiting Boston, is a son of Lady
Wilde, a poetical contributor thirty years ago
to the Dublin Nation. He stands siz feel one
in his stockings, --and irreverent exchange re-
marks that he is a good deal taller than his
poetry. At his lecture in Music Hall the other
evening he wore clothes like a man, and the
sixty Harvard student occupying front seats
in bicycle stockings and bearing sunflowers,
"who came to scoff remained to" --get rather
the worst of it; for Oscar, having had wind of
their intentions, in a little prelude turned the
laugh on them. --EX.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE CONVENTION.

	    STATE OF NEW YORK

	    	  CONCLUDED.

  Having two or three hours on our hands be-
fore the evening lecture, we (Sister Young,
Dr. F. and myself,) went up to the Fifth Ave-
nue Hotel, where on a luxurious sofa we pleas-
antly spent the hours of intermission inter-
changing thought by reviewing the proceedings
of the day.
  The evening hour drawing near, we went
early, that we might see Miss Anthony and re-
ceive a book purchased after the morning ses-
sion.  We had a few moments of very inter-
esting conversation with her upon several
points, especially upon Utah representation on
the Suffrage question, the history of which she
was anxious to secure to insert in the coming
volume of suffrage history.  Her prompt re-
quest of us to name some one to whom she
could apply for the Anti-Mormon account was
so markedly in contrast to the interest usually
manifested in hearing the Mormon side, that I
could not refrain from drawing her attention
to the fact right there, by saying: "Were you
always so eager to hear our side on all matters
of dispute concerning us as you are the anti-
side, we would suffer a great deal less from
misrepresentation than we now do.  The direct,
telling truth and the appropriateness of the re-
mark were appreciated by her and for a moment
she seemed a little nonplussed, but soon rallied
and made some remark concerning how carefully
they had to guard the cause by shunning even
 the appearance of evil; which seemed to be
the interpretation of allowing Mromon women
to be represented on the platform, especially
now in the present feverish state of society.
She also very truthfully said, that at Washing-
ton it was very different, and we could be rep-
resented there, and that though she had not a
shadow of a belief in the principle of Plural
Marriage, she had no schism on the suffrage
question.  For our part we felt there is suffi-
cient truth in our cause to preserve it and let
it bide its time, without asking for opportunity
to tell our story,
   The evening session was occupied by Mrs.
Gougar, of Lafayette, Indiana, Phebe Hanni-
ford, and appropriately wound up by one of
the finishing touches of the convention, a
speech by Miss Susan B. Anthony.
   Mrs. Gougar is editor of Our Herald, the
principal exponent of suffrage now that Mrs.
Gage has relinquished her paper.  Mrs. Gon-
gar is a most pleasing appearing woman, and a
very graceful speaker.  Her speech was par-
ticularly characterized by good, practical, com-
mon sense and convincing argument, and pos-
sessed the one simple, strong point of truth.
   The next speaker was Mrs. Phebe Hanni-
ford, a minister.  Her sweet, placid face, and
the soft, liquid fire of her great, dark eyes,
attested the earnestness with which she engages
in any conflict for truth's sake.  Her speech
was impressive, but brief, and its crowning dif-
ferentiating feature from all the other speeches
was that she asked for the fruition of  her
hopes and desires for the cause, through the
name of the Lord Jesus.  Why did not all
do so?
   Miss Susan B. Anthony now took the plat-
form, as a fitting apostle to close the conven-
tion.  Now everybody who is not as mean and
green with prejudice and jealousy, as a tomato
worm, cannot help admiring and liking Miss
Anthony.  If she is terse and decided, and hits
the nail a peeling clip square on the top of the
head, is not that the way to do, when we are
in dead earnest to accomplish anything?  The
sacred Book says there is a time for everything,
and there are certainly many times in one's
life when it is a time of not mincing matters.

[Column 2]
She is thoroughly convinced that it is next to
impossible to get the masses of people to do
something of which they know nothing--that
is, voting for the franchise of woman.  Though
thus morally convinced of the little to be ac-
complished by lecturing to the masses, she
heroically intends to keep on trying.  We
were forced to leave during her lecture, but I
can assure you nothing short of the vivid pic-
ture of having to walk all the way to the ferry,
and swimming East River, a mile wide, and
walking two miles with our dresses fringed
with icicles, could have raised us from our
seats to leave her most interesting lecture.
   If I understood Miss Anthony aright, she
carried the idea that we had never made but
one regular effort at representation, and that
was when yourself and Mrs. Zina Young Wil-
liams went to Washington.  The truth is, that
the characteristic appreciation of the daugh-
ters of Zion of the true key stone of power
and strength, in the foundation of any commu-
nity or nation, is well expressed in the proverb
of "Charity begins at home," and they have
been so husily engaged in cleaning up the
House of Zion by teaching, encouraging and
endeavoring to direct in the best channels the
thoughts and lives of their sisters, that seem-
ingly the fact has not been considered that
there might be safety in having a few sentinels
stationed on the towers afar off.   I presume it
is true of woman's cause as of any other, that
to have its proper standing requires well-
directed representation.
   One little experience of Sister Zina D. H.
Young shows the actual power of the saying,
"Seeing is believing."  As she was standing
waiting to sign the memorial to send  the Leg-
islature, asking for the franchise of the women
of New York State, a lady close by her thus
addressed her: "I believe you are Mrs. Young."
Sister Young said, "I am."  And thereupon
the lady withdrew herself a pace, and, while
Aunt Zina stood there in her sealskin sacque
and velvet robe, but above all with the light
of truth beaming forth from every feature of
her face, she took a scrutinizing survey of her
from top to toe, and said, in accents of supreme
surprise, "Why, you do not look very degrad-
ed!"  The woman seemed totally oblivious to
the burning insult her every word, act and
look implied, but seemed wholly swallowed up
with her discovery.  And I dare say dear,
good Aunt Zina bore this with a smile; but it
has been proven, as Fanny Fern says, that you
can smile when you are the maddest.  But it
is to be sincerely hoped that such insults shall
not be so frequent as to induce in any one a
"chronic smile," a la Colfax.
   My attendance at the convention was quite
a digression on my part from my daily attend-
ance at the Eye and Ear Infirmary.  Rain,
snow, storm or sleet are mere feathers before
my all-devouring interest in this field of study.
When it is considered that the whole life of
many eminent men has been spent in the study
of the eye, and yet leaving many points open
for investigation, some idea can be formed of
the vastness of the field of inquiry afforded by
so small an organ. I feel to acknowledge the
hand of the Lord in the inspiration which
prompted my visit East this winter, for I
seemed to be ripe for the harvest; for I know
that I have gained a more comprehensive grasp
of the vast subject, and dipped deeper into the
minutiae of it, during these three months, than
I possibly could have done in years alone.  I
only regret that I cannot stay longer, but the
home notes sound loud in my heart, and I must
return at latest by the middle of May.  I
have given the ear of throat also close atten-
tion, for our climate or country would seem to
be predisposing to all these diseases, judging
from the number afflicted.
   I will not indulge in any platitudes of how
I long to be at home with you again, for just
as true as twice one in two, any true Latter-
day Saint woman, when she turns her back
upon Zion, has to possess more will force than
the wife of Lot had to keep her from looking
back all the time.  I pray for you all, but my
deepest and strongest prayer at present is for
myself; and I hope my friends securely at
home will also remember me in theirs, that I
may again soon be safe at home in Zion, for I
fully realize the strength of Zion is sufficient
for the day, and if all men fail, the One at the
helm will not.
		Affectionately yours,
				ROMANIA B. PRATT.
   292 Carlton Ave., Brooklyn, Feb., 4, 1882.


ANSWER TO SOME FACTS.

SOME facts have been given of the state of
affairs in the States. Here are the facts that have
occured in Utah among the despised Latter-
day Saints. Here I am aquainted with many
noble women, who of their own free will have
given other wives unto their husbands, who are
a blessing to their families and to their associ-
ates. I have stood by the grave of a dear
friend, a first wife, and when the coffin was
lowered have seen the second wife clinging to
her husband weeping in bitterness of grief, for
the loss of the first wife, who had been such a
comfort and blessing to her and her children.
	I have known of childless first wives, imitat-
ing the example of the peerless Sarah, giving
other wives to their husbands, and furthermore
caring for the children as if they were their
own.
	I have been acquainted with second wives
who have willingly given their first born to
the childless, first wife, who has nourished and
raised it on her own.
	I have known of the first wives treating the
wives of their husbands as daughters affection-
ately and kindly, because they were younger
than therself; and again second wives honoring
and respecting the first wife in every possible
way.
	At the funeral of a second wife, I have seen
the first and third wives mourning the loss of
the one departed and knew that their hands
had helped to array the silent form in spotless
white, and placed within the marble fingers the
sweet, white flowers.
	In sickness one wife will minister to another
with the greatest solicitude and tenderness.
	Another, in a time of sickness of her husband
and second family has provided bread for
them to eat from her own resources, although
she had a large family, of her own, and had
always helped the second wife in raising her
family, because of her ill health.
	I was acquainted with a lovely woman, who
on her death bed, chose a wife for her husband
and when she found the parties were agreeable
to her wishes, felt satisfied, knowing in whose
hands she would leave her children.
	Another estimable woman while she lived
took care of the aged second wife, surrounding
her with every comfort in her helplessness, but,
although younger, passed a way first.
	These are some of the fruits of plural mar-
riage and are they evil?
	"By their fruits ye shall know them" whether
good or bad.
EMILY B. SPENCER

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE TERRIBLE MORMONS.

"Who hath believed out report? And to whom hath
the arm of the Lord been revealed?

Ah! wherefore these sounds from afar?
This tempest of wrath in our ears?
The hisses of hate, and the fury of War-
Such whisp'ring of horrible fears?
From abroad, and at home on the winds sweeping by-
Is wafted the woful, and wearisome cry.

Ah! wherefore the Nations distress?
What calamity crosses their path?
Are legions their foes-even giants-no less-
Mighty men, than Goliath of Gath?
What danger impending creates such dismay-
That the civilized world are in battle array?

In the Vales of the mountainous West-
A sprinkling of people reside-
A handful at most-who believingly boast-
That the Lord is their friend and their guide.
"Gainst these "Terrible Mormons" are vengefully hurl'd
The malice of millions, the wrath of a world.

And why do the multitude sit
On a few, in such judgement and ire?
More crimes than the masses, do Mormons commit-
Provoking a tumult so dire?
"Away with such people" tis bigotry's cry;
Twas thus the Jews clamor'd, that Jesus should die.

A Seer, in the centuries past-
Had a vision, 'twas vivid and grand;
Its substance was this, that Jehovah at last
Would set up His rule in the land.
In the midst of the mountains, a people forsooth-
Should life up an Ensign, the bammer of Truth!

Did Daniel the dreamer, the Jew-
Ever see what a state, what a time,
Of trouble there'd be should his vision come true?
 No matter what age or what clime
Should God found a Kingdom, did Daniel foretell,
"Twould be so vexatious to earth and to hell?

Yea, the prophets foretold that the world
Would speak "gainst the Holy and High,"
Would war with His Saints, and encompass their camps
And in conflict, Jehovah defy-
That dominion and triumph at length should be given
To the Saints, who shall serve in the battle of heaven."

At present, "The Stone" is so small
That's destined to roll and to grow.
Why the Nations are troubled at all-
Is more than the half of them know;
Oh is't the conviction, the positive dread
That Truth so Eternal, must prosper and spread?

The "Terrible Mormons will keep-
On the track that they've started to go,
They labor to live, yea they plant and they reep-
Untroubled, for surely they know-
That He whose commandments they dare to obey
Doth care for His Kingdom, by night, and by day.
					EMILY HILL WOODMANSEE.
	S. L. City.


A MOTHER'S TESTIMONY

MY DEAR LULA:
	I have been reflecting upon the general sit-
uation of the Latter-day Saints and their ac-
cusers. Why is it that we have not more faith?
We who have cried unto the Lord, and had
our petitions granted time after time, how is it
that we cannot have faith sufficient to accom-
plish any and every righteous undertaking?
It is perhaps, not expedient that we should at
present, exercise that divine gift to the extent
that some of us might desire. Otherwise, I
think it would be given to us in a greater de-
gree than is yet manifested among us. We
know we have no reason to fear for the Lord
is with us and He is able to accomplish His
own purposes.
	It is a source of very great happiness to me,
that all my children are on the Lord's side of
the battle in this terrible day of calamitous

[Column 2]
events. How merciful the Lord has been to
me in answering my prayers in this matter!
For when my children were small and I used
to bathe their little bodies, it was my continual
prayer that God would keep them from sin,
cleanse their hearts and make them brave to
battle for the right and to over-come evil, that
they might be worthy to dwell with Him in His
Kingdom. I believe that each one of my sons
and daughters are now striving to serve the
Lord and lay up treasures in heaven. Have I
not reason to rejoice and praise God? I feel
like bearing my testimony to the truth of the
Latter-day work, with a desire that the same
may abide with my children and my children's
children forever.
	I had the great privilege of knowing Jos-
eph Smith personally; and I know for myself
and not for another that he was a true prophet
of the only true and living God. I know that
every principle brought forth and established
by him is of God, and that the Lord will sup-
port and sustain those who will be faithful and
diligent in upholding and defending those prin-
ciples.
	The enemies of all righteousness are blinded
in regard to the plan of salvation and the re-
demption wrought out by the atoning blood of
Christ. Hence they wage war against those
who would meekly strive to follow out the pat-
tern given by Jesus, and execute the purposes
of His Father upon the earth. They complain
of the so called"Mormons" because they make
covenants with the Lord by sacrifice and re-
ceive the blessings which follow such works.
It is something of which they are entirely ignor-
ant, and into which they are determined not
to get an insight; but still they must complain
and strive to get control of the people who are
seeking to serve God that they may prohibit
the building up of His Kingdom upon the
earth. But they will be disappointed in their
designs as those people were  sought to
take away the rights of the Prophet Daniel, so
that he should not even pray to the Lord whom
he served. Did those people believe in God
or in His prohpet? They had the same respect
for holy beings and sacred things that our per-
secutors have to-day. What did they accom-
plish? Their own destruction! And those who
are seeking to destroy the Latter-day Saints
and the Priesthood of God from off the earth
today, will accomplish the same thing from them-
selves.
	My children be true to God and to your-
selves. Shrink not from any duty however
dark the way may appear before you. Put
your trust in God, knowing that this life is of
but little moment compared to that which
awaits the faithful followers of Jesus Christ,

	"Eye's not seen the untold treasured,
	Which the Father hath in story,
	Teeming with surpassing pleasures,
	Even life for evermore!"

	Your father joins me in best love and con-
stant prayers for your present and eternal wel-
fare.
		Your affectionate mother,
					Susan K. Greene


SELF GOVERNMENT

Self-government is the power of controlloing
the mind; a person exercising it must accord-
ing to phrenology, possess a great amount of
intellectuality. The mind of man, like a gar-
den, must be cultured and pruned; the soil is
never so rich but stray weeds peep forth occa-
sionally, and gaining strong hold, choke a ten-
der flower by which they chance may spring.
Self-government is the basis to all success; it is
gained by unswerving confidence in one's self
avoiding trepidation by keeping constant vigi
over--every thought, word and act; thus pre-
venting extremes, and preserving an "even
tenor of preceedure, in all transactions, men-
tal or physical. To know thyself is to be a
king - a God of the frail, mortal bark you
possess, which, if not carefully piloted, is tossed
from wave to wave on the sea of life, like a
vessel void of a helmsman. A man possessed
of self-government is referred to by his imme-
diate acquaintances as the embodiment of man-
liness; his every act so accords with what God
destined man should be, that he is pronounced
and acknowledged as a person of firm, resolute
determination. A forcible character, accom-
panied by gentleness, hospitality and sound
reasoning faculties, is the result of a constant
toning of the passions. How loved is one thus
endowed! His society is ever courted by those
of a genial spirit, and envied by those lacking
this fortitude of mind, whose immoral views
are based on circumstances, and whose minds
revolt at the idea of penetrating the recesses of.
a noble virtuous man's character.
Firmly anchored by self-government, the
tensions, of the brain is tested without injury;
impressions are chronicled and retained, until
the mind is an overflowing storehouse from
which the capacities draw, as they require;
universal knowledge becomes visible, and the
face its index, beams as a star when lit up
with the pure spirit of an honorable self-
willed man. His ideas, carefully adjusted and
clothed rise before us like crystal streams of
living waters issuing from a fountain, whose
particles set free, separate and revive the droop-
ing twig near by, imparting to it a refreshing
glow, and receiving for recompense the parched
leaflet's apparent recovery.
From a moral standpoint, the majority of
mankind have unconsciously drifted far from
the path of decorum; hence, to assert supremacy
over this common current, one must, as it were,
stand a  ne, and bear unfeeling, keen animad-
ver i  ms, hurled from a heartless commuuity;
with the one comforting thought that the, mas-
t r of himself, can withstand the tempter's
fiendish wiles, and will eventually be compli-
mented by those very ones who at first ridi-
culed his integrity of manhood.
While the two widely differing personages
are before us, let us scan their countenances;
one bear the likeness of his Maker; intelli-
gence, nobleness, and valor blend and send
forth an assurety of their presence by that
bold, frank, noble look. The other - what a
contrast! How wide the chasm! Wrecked
manhood and vice have left their traces; the
furrowed brow and tainted air tell their own
story; the sullen eyes shrink from the steady
gaze of the former, and too late he finds him-
self a slave. No one can depend on him; his
word is naught; he is liable to change with the
first ebbing tide; like driftwood, he is floating
down the stream of life, to lodge against the
first obstacle; while the self-possessed man
moves in society that his deportment com-
mands. He is honored and sought for counsel,
is a nucleus round which many rally for sup-
port; thus, endowed with capabilities, he be-
comes a ruler. What a marked affinity be-
tween such a character and out God, who evi-
dently, first learned to govern Himself, ere He
entrusted in His care, our earth. Reflect, O
ye Christians! how great will be your joy
when you appear at the immortal bar for judg-
ment, if those life-giving words greet your ears,
"Well done, thou good and faithful servant."
This we can only meet by having had con-
stantly for our motto, "Know thyself." In
conformity to the example portrayed by our
Father in Heaven, and as architects of our
own characters, it becomes incumbent, as obe-
dient children, to lay as the foundation stone -
Self-government.
					JENNIE HARRIS.
	B.Y. Academy, Provo, Feb. 12, 1882.
